content.title: Fig. 1. Trends in GDP, energy consumption, and population, as well as emissions of  SO_2 ,  NO_x , primary  PM_{2.5} , non-methane volatile organic compounds (NMVOCs), and  CO_2  between 1990 and 2017, with values for 1990 taken as 1. GDP, energy consumption, and population data were obtained from the National Bureau of Statistics of China. Emission data of air pollutants ( SO_2 ,  NO_x ,  PM_{2.5} , and NMVOCs) for 2010-2017 was obtained from Tsinghua University's multi-resolution emission inventory for China (MEIC) [5].  CO_2  emission data was obtained from the International Energy Agency (IEA) [6].
content.summary: The figure illustrates trends in GDP, energy consumption, population, and air pollutant emissions in China from 1990 to 2017, highlighting changes relative to 1990 levels.
content.insight: The figure highlights the increase in economic activity, energy consumption, and emissions, underscoring the challenge of balancing economic growth and environmental protection.

The sources for  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  are distributed across multiple sectors involving high-temperature combustions, resulting in increased difficulty and higher costs for  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  emission mitigation. In addition to coal combustion processes, including thermal power and industrial plants, on-road vehicles and other fossil fuel combustion processes are important sources of  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions. Total  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions increased threefold during 1990-2010 (Fig. 1), as indicated by both bottom-up estimation and satellite observations [24]. Many large cities have experienced rapid motorization since 2000, resulting in their urban nitrogen dioxide  $(\\mathrm{NO}_2)$  concentrations exceeding NAAQS. Increasing trends in the ratios of nitrate  $(\\mathrm{NO}_3^-)$  to sulfate  $(\\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-})$  in both ambient  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  mass [25] and precipitation [10] have been observed in many regions of China. metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_22", "source_md": "Air quality changes in China 2013-2020_ Effectiven.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Air quality changes in China 2013-2020_ Effectiven\\figure_1.jpg", "title": "Fig. 1. Timeline of CCTPs implemented in China during 2015-2020. (Note: the public information is collected from the National Energy Administration, and the illustration is made by the authors).", "references": ["On the policy side, the Chinese government explicitly proposed to promote the clean and efficient use of coal in 2014. And subsequently, two significant specialized action plans were published in 2015 (Action Plan for Clean and Efficient Use of Coal in Industry and Action Plan for Clean and Efficient Use of Coal), signifying that the clean and efficient use of coal in China has entered the implementation stage since 2015 (Zhu, 2019). Herein, the former plan chose four coal-dominated industries as key objects, and defined 21 types of critical technologies for clean and efficient coal utilization in the industry; the latter plan proposed the subjects involved in CCTPs include the government, enterprises, market, and the public, and all of the coal-rich districts in China were selected as key regions. Currently, a series of laws, regulations, and policies have been brought into effect, to alleviate air pollution and accelerate the development of CCTs. Additionally, China's 13th Five-Year Plan (FYP) ranges from 2016 to 2020, which sets goals and directions for national economic and social development during this period. In response to the economic regulation and marker supervision, 2020 is set as the last implementation year of many CCTPs, as shown in Fig. 1. Overall, these policies directed the dominant logic of atmospheric administration in the 13th FYP, namely utilizing coal resources cleanly and efficiently, reducing emissions comprehensively, and decreasing coal consumption."], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Introduction & Background", "sub_roles": ["Introduce Background Knowledge"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Diagrams & Schematics", "sub_types": ["Flowchart"]}, "key_entities": ["CCTPs", "National Energy Administration", "Action Plan for Clean and Efficient Use of Coal", "13th Five-Year Plan", "Energy Technological Revolution", "Chemical Industry Innovation"], "concise_summary": "Timeline illustrating CCTPs implemented in China from 2015 to 2020, highlighting key initiatives and action plans aimed at the clean and efficient use of coal.", "inferred_insight": "The figure outlines major policy and action plans to utilize coal resources cleanly, reduce emissions, and align with China's 13th Five-Year Plan goals, shaping the atmospheric administration strategy.", "paper_id": 4}

content.title: Fig. 2. Heating area and coal consumption (1997-2018).
content.summary: Fig. 2 depicts a timeline of policies related to air pollution control and energy transition from coal to cleaner alternatives in Northern China from 1963 to 2021.
content.insight: The figure illustrates the progressive implementation of domestic and foreign policies aimed at reducing coal consumption and improving air quality, culminating in the 'Winter Clean Heating Plan' for 2017-2021.

metadata.full_json:
{"id": "img_69", "source_md": "Does energy transition improve air quality_ Eviden.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\Does energy transition improve air quality_ Eviden\figure_2.jpg", "title": "Fig. 2. Heating area and coal consumption (1997-2018).", "references": ["Due to the lower temperatures in Northern China, burning coal for heating had become the most common way in which residents kept warm in winter. Since the 1950s, the Government of China provided winter heating via coal-fired boilers in the north Huai River areas. Northern cities received free unlimited heating from November 15 to March 15. Up until now, heating has not only been an important indicator of social wealth but has also become necessary for the survival of northern people in winter. According to statistical data released by the Ministry of Housing and Urban-Rural Development (MOHURD) of the People's Republic of China, the annual growth rate of the urban centralized heating area reached  $14.04\%$  during 1991-2017 (Fig. 2). At the same time, coal consumption increased under the rising demand for heating in the two decades prior to 2013. The "coal-to-gas/electricity" policy has generally altered the previous energy consumption structure. However, the rural heating energy consumption structure has largely remained unbalanced, among which coal consumption accounts for approximately  $75\%$ . Moreover, the large scattered coal heating demand has caused a series of environmental and social problems. The combustion of coal in boilers is associated with the release of air pollutants, especially  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  and total suspended particles (TSP). Winter heating has also reduced people's life expectancy in northern regions of China [27]. To meet the increasing demand for heating as well as to alleviate air pollutant emissions, the National Development and Reform Commission of the People's Republic of China announced its plan for the WCHP project (covering 2017-2021).", "implementing intervention measures have seen substantial air quality improvements [7,8]. For example, the United States of America has replaced coal with natural gas in generating electric power since 1997 and has achieved  $44\%$  and  $40\%$  reduction in  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  and  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  emissions from the 1997 levels [9]. A cleaner heating technologies program in New Zealand has led to the significant decline of PM emissions and concentrations [10]. Such policies aiming to diversify energy structures with clean and renewable energy have been successful in certain countries, which also has important implications for China [11]. In Northern China, the annual consumption of coal used for heating is approximately 400 million metric tons, nearly  $50\%$  of which is separately distributed for use in rural areas. Moreover, the combustion of scattered coal heating has caused severe haze to occur in winter [12]. Chen et al. found that the combustion of scattered coal heating has led to an approximately two-thirds increase in air pollutants during periods when heating is employed (i.e., winter) compared to periods when heating is not employed (i.e., summer) [13]. Compared to coal, clean energies such as natural gas and wind power emit less  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , and  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ , and thus reduce human health risks from exposure to air pollutants [9]. Therefore, switching from scattered coal", "Studies on the effectiveness of energy transition have not produced a consensus among scholars. Lin and Zhu [18] found that fuel switching in "energy saving and emission reduction" (ESER) policy had failed to decrease  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations. Li and Chen [19] reported that a city that underwent coal-to-gas transition had no significant impact on reducing  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions, and this was primarily because the city focused on the replacement of industrial coal while ignoring the combustion of scattered coal heating in rural areas. Additionally, although Zhao and Luo [20] reported that a "coal-to-gas" project decreased pollutant emissions from a household sector by substituting civil urban coal consumption, they confirmed that this project had a limited effect on reducing industrial coal consumption. Liu et al. [21] adopted the GAINS model to examine the effect of coal switching strategy in Jing-Jin-Ji region, and estimated that the primary  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  decreased by  $28\%$  and  $11\%$ , respectively. Wu et al. [22] evaluated the household coal-replacement program in North China and concluded that coal-to-electricity policy achieved the goal of a clean winter, while the high-quality coal replacement policy had no improvement in indoor air quality. Since the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (smog) crisis in the winter of 2013, environmental governance in China has shifted attention to scattered coal heating in rural areas, after which a series of policies were implemented and subsequently achieved remarkable results [23]. However, official reports may have overestimated the benefits of the clean heating policy by simply attributing the decrease in air pollutant concentrations to the promotion of clean heating policies while ignoring the interference of other exogenous energy-saving and emission-reduction policies [24]. Additionally, policy impacts will vary in different regions; thus, it is necessary to identify the validity of such policy among regions. Few studies to date have focused on the evaluation of energy transition policies in China. As an important part of China's energy transition process, it must be determined that whether or not the "Winter Clean Heating Pilot" (WCHP) project improves air quality. Answering this question can offer some guidance in China's future environmental governance while also providing a quantitative reference for other coal-based, energy-consuming developing countries. It could also be beneficial to achieve the synergy between emission reduction and air quality improvement under the background of climate change.", "On the one hand, even though the WCHP project has proven to be an effective mean to control winter-derived heating emissions, the cost of this project should not be ignored. The conversion cost of "coal-to-gas/electricity" is higher and includes a subsequent increase in the cost of gas. Replacing unprocessed coal with clean coal can be used as a transitional method in the ultimate goal of energy transition for regions that experience considerable financial governmental pressure and low residential income. The government should focus on reforming the cost of electricity/gas by updating to more advanced heating technology and devices. On the other hand, since the reduction effect of the WCHP project on regional air quality varies under different development levels and resource distributions, project control should be delegated to regional governments based on local features rather than following the outdated pattern of "one-size-fits-all". In order to minimize the negative impact of clean heating policies on energy poverty and energy consumption inequality, the government should provide subsidies to clean energy more affordable to low-income groups.", "[1] Pope lii CA, Burnett RT, Thun MJ, Calle EE, Krewski D, Ito K, et al. content.title: Fig. 2. Comparisons of emission uncertainties of coal-fired power plants in Guizhou in 2005 using sector-based and unit-based methods. The “+” represents the central value and the box represents the  95\%  CI using the unit-based method, while the larger range line represents the  95\%  CI using the sector-based method.
content.summary: Figure 2 compares emissions uncertainties of coal-fired power plants in Guizhou, 2005, using sector-based and unit-based methods. Central values and 95% CIs are shown for each pollutant.
content.insight: The figure demonstrates that using a unit-based method reduces emission uncertainty compared to the sector-based approach, highlighting the importance of detailed source information.

Results showed that the most significant decrease in AQI occurred in winter, with average rates of  $35.46\\%$ ,  $30.23\\%$ ,  $29.22\\%$ ,  $25.90\\%$ ,  $24.25\\%$ ,  $22.37\\%$  and  $6.63\\%$  for the northeast, southern, central, eastern, northern, southwest, and northwest China, respectively. The AQI decrease in autumn was also substantial, and northeast China had the greatest decline, averaging  $35.77\\%$  after the implementation of CCTPs. content.title: Fig. 5. AQI regional spatial autocorrelation in 31 provincial cities of the Chinese mainland.
content.summary: The figure displays annual spatial autocorrelation of AQI in 31 Chinese provincial cities from 2013 to 2020, highlighting high-high and low-low pollution areas.
content.insight: The figure illustrates regional AQI patterns, showing reduced pollution in northern China post-policy implementation, supporting the effectiveness of air quality improvements.

metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_26", "source_md": "Air quality changes in China 2013-2020_ Effectiven.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Air quality changes in China 2013-2020_ Effectiven\\figure_5.jpg", "title": "Fig. 5. AQI regional spatial autocorrelation in 31 provincial cities of the Chinese mainland.", "references": ["After the spatial gatherings of air pollution are confirmed, to better identify the gathering district, a regional spatial autocorrelation analysis is also conducted. As shown in Fig. 5, there were still high-high gathering areas (high levels of pollution) and low-low gathering areas (low levels of pollution) from 2013 to 2020. Meanwhile, a north-south spatial heterogeneity pattern was obvious, depicting the high levels of pollution in northern China and low levels of pollution in southern China.", "Clean and efficient use of coal in China entered the implementation stage in 2015, and a series of policies were established to promote the sustainable development of clean coal technologies (CCTs). However, the long-term effects of the clean coal technology policies (CCTPs) have not been evaluated, and the outbreak of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) would bring great uncertainty regarding the effectiveness evaluation. Employing deep learning approaches and spatial autocorrelation analysis, the present work intended to explore the air quality variation before and after the implementation of CCTPs, and investigate the association between air quality and socioeconomic factors to explore the internal mechanism of air quality improvement. Results showed that after implementing CCTPs, the air quality index (AQI) had an average reduction of  $18.82\\%$  and the identical drop in air pollution in 2018 implied a 2-year time lag of CCTPs. Additionally, emission reduction and deindustrialization were explored as two promising ways to improve air quality while promoting energy transformation. The findings indicated that the Chinese government ought to pay more attention to long-term plans; industrial restructuring and environmental protection should be insisted upon to realize sustainable energy development. By providing a perspective on air quality improvement after the policies are implemented, this study can lead practitioners and academia to a comprehensive and objective view of clean coal policies.", "To accelerate energy transformation and reduce air pollution, both central and provincial governments in China are taking increasingly leading roles in shaping policy outcomes, through adopting cleaner production or energy saving policies, and revisions of local standards or emission reduction. Since the Chinese energy structure is characterized by being rich in coal, poor in oil, and lack of gas, thus a series of coal-togas (CTG) and coal-to-electricity (CTE) policies are established to promote the clean conversion of coal resources. Further, coal-fired stoves were the most popular source of heating in winter in Northern China, which are cheap and easily available, however, would lead to heavy emissions of various air contaminants (Fan et al., 2020). Strategies to control residential coal combustion (RCC) have been a focus since 2016. Accordingly, there is growing attention from researchers to examine the effectiveness of RCC, CTG, and CTE policies (Xue et al., 2016; Chen and Chen, 2019; Chen et al., 2022). Applying a comprehensive perspective of economic, energy, and environmental impact, Lin and Jia (2020) researched that the CTE policy is an effective pattern to eliminate  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  and  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  emissions, more than half of residents' emissions could be cut down, and the co-benefit between CTE and other energy saving policies was observed. As for the implementation of the RCC policy, Li et al. (2018) estimated that air quality in Beijing would be remarkably improved if residential coal in the BTH region and the surrounding areas are replaced by other clean energy sources. Similar results have been obtained in Xue et al.'s research (2016) as well, using scenario analysis, the projected emissions of primary pollutants from RCC showed that it is difficult to eliminate the emissions from residential sources only rely on mandatory control measures, adopting multiple kinds of policy instruments to promote the clean conversion of coal resources is a preferable pathway (Xue et al., 2016). Yu et al. (2021) examined the dynamic effects of CTG policy and its impact mechanism on air pollution, revealing that this policy has significantly improved air quality in 274 Chinese prefecture-level cities, with a reduction of  $31.3\\%$  for industrial  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $36\\%$  for industrial smoke, and  $33.1\\%$  for AQI.", "The Chinese mainland is composed of 33 administrative regions, however, the policies implemented by Hong Kong and Macao special administrative regions differ from the other provinces. Moreover, coal-dominated factories and enterprises are mainly located in the capital city of these provinces, which are typical epitomes of regional environmental pollution. Therefore, a total of 31 provincial capital cities in the Chinese mainland are selected. The system architecture of this study is depicted in Fig. 2.", "To further analyze the temporal and spatial variation features of air quality in 31 provincial capital cities from 2013 to 2020, the spatial autocorrelation analysis method is applied. ArcGIS 10.6 and GeoDa software is used for the calculation and analytic process.", "The study obtained the data including a total of 1440 daily air quality and meteorological data (January 22, 2016, to December 31, 2019) from 31 provincial capital cities in the Chinese mainland,  $60\\%$  of which are chosen as the training set,  $20\\%$  as the validation set and  $20\\%$  as the test set. After determining the best basic network architecture for the current prediction tasks, models are set up in units of cities, and the training set is used to train the tailored CNN-LSTM models. The accuracy indicators of the model prediction results of 31 cities, namely RMSE values averaging 22.06, models perform well overall, indicating that the applied CNN-LSTM network has good capabilities for AQI prediction (Jin et al., 2020; Mao et al., 2021).", "Global spatial autocorrelation analysis is carried out using the average monthly AQIs for the 31 studied cities from 2013 to 2020. As shown in Table 3, the results of global Moran's I are all greater than 0 and statistically significant, indicating that there are significant and positive spatial correlations among the average monthly AQIs. In summary, there is an obvious regional integration tendency of air pollution in Chinese cities."], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Geospatial Visualizations", "sub_types": ["Map"]}, "key_entities": ["AQI", "spatial autocorrelation", "Chinese mainland", "provincial cities"], "concise_summary": "The figure displays annual spatial autocorrelation of AQI in 31 Chinese provincial cities from 2013 to 2020, highlighting high-high and low-low pollution areas.", "inferred_insight": "The figure illustrates regional AQI patterns, showing reduced pollution in northern China post-policy implementation, supporting the effectiveness of air quality improvements.", "paper_id": 4}

metadata.full_json:
{"id": "img_223", "source_md": "一种工业热源提取方法_利用温度特征模板的BP神经网络.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\一种工业热源提取方法_利用温度特征模板的BP神经网络\figure_4.jpg", "title": "Fig.4 Number of industrial heat source objects and industrial heat anomaly points in Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and its surrounding areas", "supplement": "(a) 工业热源对象 (b) 工业热源热异常点 图4 京津冀及周边地区工业热源对象和工业热异常点数量 ", "references": ["Abstract: Aiming at the problem of insufficient quantity and spatial refinement in the extraction of industrial heat source from annual scale thermal anomaly data, a neural network industrial heat source extraction method based on temperature feature template is proposed by using VIIRS active fire data. This study took Beijing-Tianjin- Hebei and its surrounding areas as the study area, Firstly, according to the spatial aggregation characteristics of industrial heat sources, the heat source objects were divided by the OPTICS algorithm. Secondly, according to the thermal radiation characteristics of the heat sources, the temperature characteristic template of industrial heat sources and non- industrial heat sources were constructed. Finally, the BP neural network was used to extract industrial heat source objects using the temperature feature template and heat source statistical characteristics as parameters. The results show that: (1) the extraction precision of industrial heat source of the neural network algorithm of temperature feature template proposed in this paper reached  $96.31\%$ . Compared with time filtering and logistic regression methods, the extraction precision of industrial heat sources was improved by  $8.45\%$  and  $7.53\%$ , respectively; (2) From 2015 to 2020, the number of industrial heat sources in the six provinces and cities in Beijing-Tianjin- Hebei and its surrounding areas decreased by  $27.46\%$ . The number of industrial heat source objects and heat anomalies in Hebei Province decreased by  $8.06\%$  and  $7.44\%$  annually, respectively, which was the largest decrease compared with other provinces and cities. The", "concentration of industrial heat sources in Shandong and Tianjin increased by  $25.72\%$  and  $86.64\%$ , respectively, indicating that the industrial transformation and upgrade policies in the two places have achieved remarkable results; (3) Tangshan, Handan, Lvliang, and Changzhi accounted for  $31.37\%$  of the total industrial heat sources in the study area, which are the main cities in Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and its surrounding areas. The degree of industrial heat source accumulation and energy consumption in seven cities such as Linfen and Taiyuan was higher than those in other cities; The degree of industrial heat source accumulation and energy consumption in 11 cities such as Beijing and Zhoukou was lower than those in other cities; (4) From January to May 2020, the number of industrial heat anomalies in Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and its surrounding areas remained unchanged or increased compared with the same period in 2019 and 2021. The COVID-19 had no significant impact on the industrial heat source in the study area. The number of industrial heat anomalies in Wuhan in January and February 2020 decreased by more than  $66.67\%$  compared with that in the same period in 2019 and 2021, the number of industrial heat anomalies from March to May 2020 was lower than that in the same period of 2019. The COVID-19 has had a significant impact on industrial heat sources in Wuhan from January to May 2020. This study reflects the current situation and trend of industrial heat source development in Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and its surrounding areas, which provides a valuable reference for the formulation and adjustment of relevant policies such as reducing energy consumption and improving secondary industry concentration.", "Key words: industrial heat source; OPTICS algorithm; temperature feature template; neural network; VIIRS Active Fire; Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and its surrounding areas", "(d) Slope_NFHWH

content.title: Fig. 3. The analysis framework of the sources for energy increase in the passenger transport sector in China.
content.summary: The diagram outlines the sources of energy increase in China's passenger transport sector, detailing sub-sectors and influential factors like population and vehicle stock structure.
content.insight: The framework highlights the impact of policy measures on energy efficiency, emphasizing transportation structure adjustment and fuel economy improvements for significant energy savings.

{"id": "img_74", "source_md": "Energy growth sources and future energy-saving pot.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\Energy growth sources and future energy-saving pot\figure_3.jpg", "title": "Fig. 3. The analysis framework of the sources for energy increase in the passenger transport sector in China.", "references": ["analysis framework is proposed to consider different characteristics of traffic modes, as shown in Fig. 3. The effects of main driving factors on total passenger transport energy use are analyzed briefly. Furthermore, a specific investigation into three subsectors of passenger transport is performed in turn.", "# Energy growth sources and future energy-saving potentials in passenger transportation sector in China", "In China, energy use from passenger vehicles is increasing rapidly due to urbanization and income growth, but the contributions of transport service structure shift and energy efficiency improvement, especially future energy-saving directions are controversial and unclear because of the differences in the choices of traffic modes, which may greatly affect the analysis results and their interpretations. This study investigates the growth sources of energy use in the passenger transport sector during 1979-2017 and future energy-saving potentials by 2030 by re-examining statistical data. Results show that the transport service increase and transport structure shift led to the increase in energy use by  $665.25\%$  and  $337.22\%$ , but approximately  $14\%$  of the increased energy use was offset by energy efficiency improvements, and its effect is limited in public transportation sector, compared with private passenger vehicle sector. In public passenger transport sector, adjusting the service or volume structure may be a desirable option while in private passenger vehicle sector the policy focus is more on improving fuel efficiency and reducing average annual kilometers traveled per capita. Overall, there is still at least  $28.3\%$  of energy reduction potential by energy efficiency improvement before 2030 compared to 2017 baseline level. Potential evaluation showed that the current potential of transport service structure adjustment based on industrial planning is limited, which highlights the necessity of reform, involving promoting the sharing transportation, railway, subway and buses.", "In the last two decades, the transport sector has become a major source of energy consumption worldwide. In China, transport energy use increased from 58.63 Mtce (million tons of coal equivalent) in 1995 to 421.91 Mtce in 2017, with an annual average growth rate of nearly  $10\%$ . Energy use from passenger transport dominates total transport energy use, accounting for nearly  $60\%$  in 2017. By 2030, more than 300 million new rural residents are expected to enter the cities in China, which will have a great impact on the passenger transport energy consumption. Passengers are relatively easily guided by policy measures to change their energy-using behaviors in their daily travel, thus leading to low-carbon", "However, one of the key challenges is that the current energy statistics of passenger transportation are fragmented in China. The lack of comprehensive statistic specification causes that the data is scattered into different sectors in energy statistical system. The data does not properly cover metrics of interest for energy consumption analysis of passenger transportation. Part of the data is covered in the Transport, Storage and Post sector and Residential Consumption sector in China Energy Statistical Yearbook [3]. The former only includes the intercity public passenger transport and", "Many studies have investigated energy consumption and emissions increase in transport sector [2,5,6]. The relationship between transport energy use and socio-economic factors has also been intensively discussed using econometric models [7-11]. Various options to save energy and reduce emissions in the transport sector have been evaluated for their reduction potential, from both the technological and behavioral perspectives [1,12,13]. In transport studies, bottom-up methodology and scenario analysis were often used to study future trends, mitigation options, and reduction potential [14,15]. In particular, road transport and passenger transport have always been the hot topics for evaluating energy consumption and emissions [16,17].", "Either from an economy-wide or a sectoral perspective, the driving forces of increased energy consumption have been extensively investigated [18-20]. In the transportation sector, these studies could be summarized from two perspectives, namely, 1) the sectoral perspective, and 2) the vehicle-based perspective. From the sectoral perspective, changes in energy consumption and emission have been analyzed either for the whole transportation sector or its subsectors. Activity factors, namely Gross Domestic Product (GDP) growth, population increase, and propensity to travel, were often found to be the main drivers of growing energy consumption [21-24], which is similar to the passenger transport subsector [25-27]. Other studies have focused more on micro sections of transportation. For example, on the vehicle level, the vehicle population, fuel mix, and vehicle fuel efficiency were", "With accelerating urbanization in China, passenger transport energy use has rapidly increased, from 28.78 Mtce in 1997 to 252.47 Mtce in 2017, with an annual average growth rate of  $11.47\%$  (Fig. 1). Energy use could thus be divided into three development stages. From 1978 to 1990, the intercity public passenger transport was the main traveling mode of passengers, as the urban and the private vehicle subsectors were undeveloped. From 1990 to 2002, energy consumption of the private passenger vehicles gradually caught up with that of the intercity subsector. From 2003 to 2017, the private subsector became the major source of the passenger transport energy use. The energy intensity of passenger transport decreased continually due to technological advances, and the energy intensity of the private passenger vehicles decreased rapidly from 143.68 Tce/Mp-km (ton coal equivalent/million passenger-kilometers) in 1997 to 51.38 Tce/Mp-km in 2017, due to the policy of fuel consumption limits for passenger cars [48]. This corresponds to a huge reduction in fuel consumption. Without this reduction in energy intensity, the fuel consumption in the private passenger transport sector would have increased nearly three times greater than it is today.", "(c) Modal energy intensity  
Fig. 7. Energy consumption and passenger volume share of three modes of the urban public passenger transport, 1978-2017. Data sources: China Statistical Yearbook [4]; China Urban Construction Statistical Yearbook [69]; Statistics bulletin of transportation sector development [64]; Fuel consumption limits for passenger cars, Fuel consumption evaluation methods and targets for passenger cars [48] and the authors' personal calculation.", "The data includes socio-economic activity, transport structure and energy intensity. The socio-economic activity data includes the population, the number of households and the GDP, which are from the China Statistical Yearbook [4]; the transport-related activity data includes the passenger service of the intercity public passenger transport, the passenger volume of the urban public passenger transport and the vehicle population of the private passenger vehicles, which are from 1) Passenger Kilometers of the Transport, Postal and Telecommunication Services, and Software Industry, 2) Basic Statistics on Public Transportation in Cities by Region of the Urban, Rural and Regional Development, 3) Possession of Private Vehicles of the Transport, Postal and Telecommunication Services, and Software Industry in the China Statistical Yearbook [4]. The structure data consists of the service structure of the intercity public modes, the volume structure of the urban public modes and the vehicle type structure of the private subsector, which are calculated based on the above transport-related activity data. The intensity data includes the energy intensity of the three subsectors and the average annual mileage of the private passenger vehicles, which are from the Yearbook of China Integrated transport [63], the Statistics bulletin of transportation sector development [64], the Railway Statistics [65], and the Fuel consumption limits for passenger cars [48]. The average annual mileage of the private passenger vehicles is from the survey data in the FEEI model [49].", "The GDP per capita effect was the dominant contributor to energy consumption growth and its contribution increased over time, but the contribution decreased during 1996-2000 (Figs. 5b) and 2008-2010 (Fig. 5c) due to the Asian financial crisis and the global financial crisis respectively. The shift of various modes of passenger transport (structure effect) contributed to the energy consumption decrease in the years leading up to 1990 because the passenger service share of civil aviation decreased (Fig. 5c). Since 1990, the structure effect turned from a negative value to a positive value. After 1990, the highway and civil aviation services were promoted vigorously by the State Council and Civil Aviation Administration of China [50,66]. Therefore, adjusting the transport structure in the intercity subsector, such as promoting the transfer form highway and civil aviation to railway, could be an effective way to save energy. The passenger service intensity effect provides an increasingly negative contribution to the increase in energy consumption during 1978-2017. The energy intensity of the railway, waterway, and civil aviation services decreased rapidly due to technological advancements and improved management.", "The GDP per capita effect was the dominant contributor to energy consumption growth due to rapid economic development in China. The most prominent contribution appeared during 1991-1995 and 2006-2010, with an energy consumption increase of  $275\%$  and  $195\%$ , respectively (Fig. 6b). The passenger volume structure effect was another main driver of the energy consumption growth due to the shift in the dominant mode of transport. From the late 1980s to the 1990s, the taxi industry grew rapidly, owing to the policy of open business implemented by the State Council", "The multiplication decomposition results in the three subsectors are shown in Fig. 9. Five influential factors on energy consumption can be observed and are quite similar in their effects in the intercity and urban passenger transport subsectors. The GDP per capita increase was always the dominant contributor to rises in energy consumption, but this effect was larger in the urban subsector (2.713). The passenger volume/service per GDP played a critical role in reducing energy consumption as opposed to energy intensity in the public transportation sector. In contrast, however, the private transport subsector showed that fuel efficiency improvement, decline of family size and average annual kilometers per capita offset the effect of vehicle stock growth per capita together.", "This study contributes to understanding the underlying driving factors of energy growth and exploring energy efficiency improvement opportunities in passenger transport sector in China. First, a comprehensive picture of passenger transport energy consumption trends during 1979-2017 in China is given. Main drivers of energy growth are briefly identified through re-classifying the fragmented data statistics, which, to our knowledge, are unique and original. Second, the effects of the main driving forces of increased energy consumption are investigated firstly by using a hybrid decomposition method. Third, the future energy-saving potentials of different policy pathways until 2020 and 2030 are estimated and compared involving sharing transportation, structure adjustment, energy efficiency improvement, and urban car ownership control.", "The results show that there are diversified contributors to energy consumption depending on the traffic modes utilized. On the whole, GDP growth per capita, motorization level and shifts in service structure mainly contribute to energy consumption increase in passenger transport sector during the period 1979-2017, and they cause more than 10 times increase in energy consumption, but the actual energy consumption increased just by 8.77 times because of the effect of energy efficiency improvement. For the intercity and urban public passenger transport, the GDP growth per capita, the service or volume structure change, and population growth strongly dominate the changes in energy consumption, and the effect of energy efficiency improvement is not very significant on a relative basis. In the private passenger vehicle sector, however, the situation is significantly different. Fuel efficiency improvement, decline of family size and average annual kilometers traveled per capita together offset the positive impacts from motorization level rise and vehicle type structure change. Especially, the fuel efficiency improvement has enormously contributed to energy conservation since 2010 in China.", "Outlook analysis shows that by 2030 energy conservation potential in intercity public passenger transport sector is limited (16.6%), but there are still huge energy-saving potentials in urban public passenger transport sector (54.3%) and private passenger transport sector (34.3%). They could totally lead to 31.6% of energy consumption reduction in passenger transport sector by 2030.", "In order to reduce energy use in passenger transport sector, multiple policy directions deserve attention. In China, the motorization level could be regulated by some mandatory policies, such as the vehicle license lottery in major cities, such as Beijing and Tianjin. In future, it is essential to give priority to public transport and sharing transport, such as the subway, bus, car-sharing, and bike-sharing. Particularly, first, sharing transportation deserves more attention in China because Chinese people are more likely to accept sharing and collective ideas, and there are less regulatory restrictions in China. Second, the energy intensity reduction targets up until 2020 need to be strictly enforced, especially for fuel efficiency standards for private passenger vehicles. Third, low-intensity transport modes, such as railway, waterway, rail transit, and mini vehicles, are strongly recommended to promote the low-carbon transition.", "[1] Waisman HD, Guivarch C, Lecocq F. content.title: Fig. 1. Comprehensive traffic monitoring network in Xiaoshan District. (a) Xiaoshan District (the red area) is located in the hinterland of the YRD in China. (b) Comprehensive traffic monitoring achieves full coverage over Xiaoshan District. Each dot represents a set of comprehensive traffic monitoring facilities that can recognize license plates, traffic fluxes, vehicle-specific speed, and vehicle categories. The entire road network over Xiaoshan District is divided into 4585 road segments. Such road segments are divided into three road classes: highways (blue lines), arterial roads (pink lines), and residential streets (green lines). Map data © 2022, Gaode Map.
content.summary: The figure illustrates the comprehensive traffic monitoring network in Xiaoshan District, highlighting road classes and monitoring facilities on a map.
content.insight: The traffic monitoring network in Xiaoshan enables detailed tracking of vehicle dynamics, crucial for analyzing the impact of traffic on air quality and public health.

content.title: Fig. 1. The distributions of  NO_{x}  emission factors for coal-fired power plants and industrial boilers. The red bars are beyond the  95\%  CIs. The figures represent power generating units: (a) without LNB and bituminous combustion ( <300MW ); (b) without LNB and anthracite combustion ( <300MW ); (c) with LNB and bituminous combustion ( <300MW ); (d) with LNB and anthracite combustion ( <300MW ); (e) with tangential burner and bituminous combustion ( \geq300MW ); (f) with wall-fired burner and bituminous combustion ( \geq300MW ); (g) with tangential burner and anthracite combustion ( \geq300MW ); (h) with wall-fired burner and anthracite combustion ( \geq300MW ); (i) with W-flame burner and anthracite combustion ( \geq300MW ). The last figure (j) represents industrial grate boilers.
content.summary: Distributions of NOx emission factors for various coal-fired power plants and industrial boilers are shown, highlighting emissions beyond 95% confidence intervals.
content.insight: The figure illustrates emission factor distribution discrepancies among different types of coal-fired units, facilitating comparison and analysis based on factors like burner type and boiler capacity.

metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_192", "source_md": "Quantifying the uncertainties of a bottom-up emiss.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Quantifying the uncertainties of a bottom-up emiss\\figure_5.jpg", "title": "Fig. 5. Comparisons of emission uncertainties for different species in China from different studies, expressed as  95\\%  CIs around the central estimates.", "references": ["for 2003 instead of 2005. Moreover, open burning of biomass was not included in Ohara et al. (2007). Regarding  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ , Ohara et al. (2007) estimated a total emission of  $36.6\\mathrm{Mt}$  for China in 2003, much higher than all other estimates for 2005 and later. This large discrepancy may come mainly from the difference between the updated domestic emission factors used in this study, and the foreign emission factor database that Ohara et al. (2007) relied more upon. Figure 5 compares the resulting emission inventory uncertainties with those of other studies, none of which included Monte Carlo simulation. The estimated uncertainty ranges for  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  and  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  emissions are very similar among the available studies, while those of PM fractions of different sizes or carbonaceous species are significantly reduced. For example, the  $95\\%$  CIs of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC emissions are estimated to be  $-17\\sim 54\\%$ ,  $-25\\% \\sim 136\\%$ , and  $-40\\% \\sim 121\\%$  in this study, respectively, much lower than  $-57\\% \\sim 130\\%$ ,  $-68\\% \\sim 208\\%$ , and  $-72\\% \\sim 258\\%$  recently derived by Zhang et al. (2009d). The main reasons include: (1) the source categories in this study are more detailed, and random errors are thus significantly reduced due to the \"compensation-of-error\" mechanism (the same statistical mechanism described for the power sector comparison in Sect. 4.4); and (2) emission factors are", "Abstract. The uncertainties of a national, bottom-up inventory of Chinese emissions of anthropogenic  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$ , and particulate matter (PM) of different size classes and carbonaceous species are comprehensively quantified, for the first time, using Monte Carlo simulation. The inventory is structured by seven dominant sectors: coal-fired electric power, cement, iron and steel, other industry (boiler combustion), other industry (non-combustion processes), transportation, and residential. For each parameter related to emission factors or activity-level calculations, the uncertainties, represented as probability distributions, are either statistically fitted using results of domestic field tests or, when these are lacking, estimated based on foreign or other domestic data. The uncertainties (i.e.,  $95\\%$  confidence intervals around the central estimates) of Chinese emissions of  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$ , total PM,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , black carbon (BC), and organic carbon (OC) in 2005 are estimated to be  $-14\\% \\sim 13\\%$ ,  $-13\\% \\sim 37\\%$ ,  $-11\\% \\sim 38\\%$ ,  $-14\\% \\sim 45\\%$ ,  $-17\\% \\sim 54\\%$ ,  $-25\\% \\sim 136\\%$ , and  $-40\\% \\sim 121\\%$ , respectively. Variations at activity levels (e.g., energy consumption or industrial production) are not the main source of emission uncertainties. Due to narrow classification of source types, large sample sizes, and relatively high data quality, the coal-fired power sector is estimated to have the smallest emission uncertainties for all species except BC and OC. Due to poorer source classifications and a wider range of estimated emission factors, considerable uncertainties of  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  and PM emissions from cement production and boiler combustion in other industries are found. The probability distributions of emission factors for biomass burning, the largest source of BC and OC, are fitted based on very limited domestic field measurements, and special caution should thus be taken interpreting", "Uncertainties introduced by energy statistics and applications of non-Chinese emission factors have been estimated in previous Chinese emission inventory studies. Streets et al. (2003) applied expert judgment, in which the coefficients of variation (i.e., standard deviation divided by the mean) of the activity levels were assumed based on measures of economic development and perceived statistical quality, and those of emission factors were based on the reliability ratings of U.S. emission factors (USEPA, 2002). They estimated that the uncertainties of Chinese emissions varied from  $-12\\% \\sim 13\\%$  for  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  to  $-83\\% \\sim 495\\%$  for organic carbon (expressed as the lower and upper bounds of a  $95\\%$  confidence interval, CI, around a central estimate). To investigate discrepancies in the trends of bottom-up  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  emissions and of the  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  column by satellite, Zhang et al. (2007a) set alternative emission scenarios in which energy statistics from different sources and emission factors with different control levels were applied. They concluded that  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  emissions in east-central China in 2004 could range from 7.9 to  $9.3\\mathrm{Tg}$ , and the annual growth rate of emissions from 1995 to 2004 could range from  $5.5\\%$  to  $7.1\\%$ . Other studies conducted preliminary uncertainty analysis of certain species or sectors in China using statistical methods. Bond et al. (2004) assumed that most emission factors of particulate matter (PM) followed lognormal distributions (with the exception of those of diesel vehicles, which had a gamma distribution) and evaluated the uncertainties of global black carbon (BC) and organic carbon (OC) emissions. The  $95\\%$  CIs for Chinese emissions were estimated to be  $-37\\% \\sim 147\\%$  and  $-44\\% \\sim 104\\%$ , respectively. Wu et al. (2010) focused on", "In this study, therefore, the uncertainties (expressed as the  $95\\%$  CI around the central estimate) of a bottom-up emission inventory in China is evaluated with Monte Carlo simulations, combining comprehensive field measurements of domestic emission factors and investigations of activity levels. The species include  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$ , total PM,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC. The target year is 2005 because it has the most available data. Determining the probability distributions of all of the related parameters is the main undertaking of this study. Section 2 reviews bottom-up emission inventory methods and analyzes the uncertainties of emission source fractions and corresponding activity levels. Section 3 is a thorough analysis of emission factors by sector and fuel type. Section 4 presents the results and related discussion of emission uncertainties by sector, sensitivity analysis, reliability and limitations of the analysis, and comparisons with other studies. Section 5 summarizes the present study.", "Chinese emissions in 2005 with  $95\\%$  CIs are shown in Table 1 by sector. To be consistent with previous studies, emissions from industrial boilers, cement plants, iron and steel plants, and other industrial processes are aggregated into a single category of industry emissions (see Table S8 in the Supplement for emissions and uncertainties in these sub-sectors). The uncertainties (expressed as the  $95\\%$  CI around the central value) are estimated to be  $-14\\% \\sim 13\\%$ ,  $-13\\% \\sim 37\\%$ ,  $-11\\% \\sim 38\\%$ ,  $-14\\% \\sim 45\\%$ ,  $-17\\% \\sim 54\\%$ ,  $-25\\% \\sim 136\\%$ , and  $-40\\% \\sim 121\\%$  for total emissions of  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$ , PM,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC respectively (see Fig. S2 for the output distributions of those species by Monte Carlo simulation).", "the result follow. The PM emission characteristics of residential coal combustion in China were tested by different researchers (Zhang and Smith, 2000; Chen et al., 2005; Zhang et al., 2008b), yielding varied emission factors. Very few estimates of the emission factors of Chinese combustion of biomass can be found, however, except for a series of measurements by Li et al. (2007a, b, 2009b). Those measurements were made in four provinces with different types of biomass. As no other domestic tests are available for direct comparisons, systematic biases in these available measurements cannot be ruled out. In this study, we adopt the results from Li et al. (2007a, b, 2009b) fully, lacking further domestic evidence. Given potential high uncertainties, however, a comparison test is conducted for OC emitted by its leading source, biomass combustion, in which the uncertainties of emission factors obtained from Li et al. (2007a, b, 2009b) are replaced with those of foreign studies (Andreae and Merlet, 2001; Streets et al., 2001; Bond et al., 2004). In this case the  $95\\%$  CI of OC emission from biomass combustion changes from  $-58\\sim 216\\%$  to  $-31\\%\\sim 311\\%$ , suggesting caution in interpretation of results based on limited domestic field measurements (see also the discussion in Sect. 4.3).", "The situations for emission factors with higher reliability (i.e., A and B) are more complicated. For coal-fired power plants, the data on which the probability distributions for most parameters are fitted are obtained from both thorough field measurements by the authors and published studies by others (see details in Zhao et al., 2010). These support different emission factors across burner types, fuel qualities, and emission control levels. The uncertainty analysis for this sector are thus the most reliable in this study. Such confidence, however, does not apply to the residential sector, particularly biomass burning, even though the emission factors meet A or B criteria. As discussed in Sect. 4.1, the data on which the distributions are based are limited in number and systematic bias may result in this sector. In other words, the reliability of uncertainty analysis of biomass burning is in fact poor despite the statistical strength of data fitting. Given its high contributions to uncertainties in emission estimates, more field measurements of emissions from biomass burning in China are urgently needed.", "Fig. 4. Comparisons of annual emissions for China from different studies. The emissions are for year 2005 unless noted. The range line represents the  $95\\%$  CIs around the central estimates of this study.", "Although inverse studies that evaluate emission inventories using observations and CTMs have indicated that Chinese  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  emissions may be underestimated by around  $50\\%$  (Wang et al., 2004; Ma et al., 2006), the uncertainty estimated in this study is relatively small, ranging only  $-13\\% \\sim 37\\%$ . The difference may result in part from omission in bottom-up inventories of anthropogenic  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  sources of entirely different character, such as microbial decomposition of organic wastes associated with the human-animal food chain and applications of chemical fertilizer (Wang et al., 2004). Moreover, emissions vary considerably across regions of China (Wang et al., 2007; Zhao and Wang, 2009) and seasons (Zhang et al., 2007a), and emission uncertainties differentiated accordingly may be larger than national estimates. In order to compare better emission estimates derived from satellite, aircraft, or surface observations, therefore, further investigation of the uncertainties of bottom-up inventories by regions and seasons is needed. These would require region-specific uncertainty assumptions for emission factors and activity levels, and seasonal distribution parameters."], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Compare Experimental Performance", "Present Quantitative Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Bar Chart"]}, "key_entities": ["emission uncertainties", "SO2", "NOx", "PM10", "PM2.5", "BC", "OC", "China", "Monte Carlo simulation"], "concise_summary": "The figure compares emission uncertainties for various species in China across different studies, showing 95% confidence intervals.", "inferred_insight": "This figure illustrates the reduced uncertainties in emission estimates for PM species and carbonaceous compounds due to improved categorizations and data quality.", "paper_id": 29}

content.title: Fig. 1. The averaged  PM_{2.5}  concentrations during APPCAP (a) and APWBSD (c), and their change in APPCAP (b), and APWBSD (d).
content.summary: The figure illustrates the average PM2.5 concentrations in China during APPCAP and APWBSD phases, showing regional variations and changes.
content.insight: The maps depict significant reductions in PM2.5 concentrations across various regions, highlighting the effectiveness of policy interventions during the APPCAP and APWBSD phases.

metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_129", "source_md": "How the Air Clean Plan and carbon mitigation measu.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\How the Air Clean Plan and carbon mitigation measu\\figure_1.jpg", "title": "Fig. 1. The averaged  PM_{2.5}  concentrations during APPCAP (a) and APWBSD (c), and their change in APPCAP (b), and APWBSD (d).", "references": ["After seven-year implementing of Air Clean Plan, the mean  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations clearly decreased  $(13.41\\mu \\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ $33.65\\%)$  in China. The average  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations in the first phase (2014-2017) were 35.97  $\\pm 15.11~\\mu \\mathrm{g~m}^{-3}$ , which decreased to  $28.92\\pm 12.76~\\mu \\mathrm{g~m}^{-3}$  in the latter phase (2018-2020) of the China ACP. In both phases, high concentrations were observed mainly in northwest China, northern China, and a small proportion of central China (i.e., Henan and Hubei provinces) (Fig. 1). The  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations decreased in over  $68.09\\%$  of the area in mainland China during the period of APPCAP (Fig. 1b). A clear reduction was detected in BTH (decreased by  $17.33~\\mu \\mathrm{g~m}^{-3}$ ;  $25.81\\%$ ), followed by YRD (decreased by  $12.79~\\mu \\mathrm{g~m}^{-3}$ ;  $24.07\\%$ ) and parts of central China (decreased by  $14.66~\\mu \\mathrm{g~m}^{-3}$ ;  $22.93\\%$ ) during the APPCAP", "Fig. 3 shows the population exposed to ambient  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in pollution control priority regions and in the whole nation in specific years at the beginning and end of the two phases of ACP (2014, 2017, and 2020). In 2014, over  $80\\%$  of Chinese people lived in areas with  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  over  $35\\mu \\mathrm{g}$ $\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$  (WHO IT-1). In 2020, the corresponding proportion decreased to  $40\\%$ . BTH had the most severe  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure, even with the most prominent air quality improvement amongst four priority regions. In 2014, almost all populations in BTH lived in areas where  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations exceeded the WHO IT-1 threshold and this population proportion decreased to  $90\\%$  in 2020. The FWP was not considered as a priority region for air pollution control in APPCAP, and pollution status was more serious during APPCAP as a result of crude industrial production and its gully terrain that was not conducive to the diffusion of pollutants. By contrast, the effect of ACP measures was profound in FWP region under APWBSD with a  $10\\%$  reduction in the population exposed to  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5} > 35\\mu \\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$  in 2020 compared to 2017. The exposed population in PRD is relatively low compared to other priority regions, with  $24\\%$  meeting the WHO IT-1 in 2014 and  $100\\%$  meeting the WHO IT-1 in 2020, which also achieved the \"Beautiful China\" vision. However, almost all populations ( $\\sim 98\\%$  in the whole nation) still lived under the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations exceeding the WHO IT-3 in 2020, not to mention the WHO AQG standard.", "During the period of APPCAP, the control policies essentially contributed to the decrease of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations (Fig. 8a). The  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  reduction was sourced primarily from the clean production policy (net decrease contribution of  $10.53\\mu \\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ), followed by the energy consumption control measure (net decrease contribution of  $7.28\\mu \\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ) and fuel structure optimization (net decrease contribution of  $3.08\\mu \\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ). Economic growth was the primary driver for increasing  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution from 2014 to 2017, with a net contribution of  $9.00\\mu \\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ . The co-effect of  $\\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in this period also intensified  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution. During the APWBSD, the contribution of clean production, energy consumption control and fuel structure optimization to  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  decrease were  $85\\%$ ,  $37\\%$  and  $21\\%$ , respectively. Compared with the period of ACPCAP, the effectiveness of all measures decreases in APWBSD, especially the clean production measures (from  $-257\\%$  to  $-85\\%$  ) and", "We provide a comprehensive estimation of the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and the number of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths, as well as spatiotemporal changes in these, and a quantitative assessment of the effectiveness of ACP methods in mainland China during 2014 to 2020. The two key contributions are: (1) We estimated the spatiotemporal variation in the number of deaths attributable to  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure in mainland China during ACP based on the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  product with the finest spatial resolution available to date, and elucidated the spatial discrepancies in policy implementation across China. China's recent clean air actions have achieved great benefits for human health and the environment. However, over  $98\\%$  of the population still lived under the risk of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution ( $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations over  $15\\mu \\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ), with 1.12 million ( $95\\%$  CI: 0.79-1.56) premature deaths caused by  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution in mainland China in 2020, accounting for  $\\sim 27.05\\%$  of global ambient  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths. In addition, the environmental and health benefits of China ACP are not fully synergistic in spatial pattern, with the greatest health benefits detected in the YRD region and the best environment benefits identified in the BTH region. To achieve the co-benefits of environment and health, specific policies need to be adopted under different conditions. (2) We quantified the environment and health co-benefits of ACP measures and carbon mitigation from 2014 to 2020. Clean production was the most effective abatement measure in China ACP, reducing net  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and net premature deaths by  $18.35\\mu \\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$  and 0.45 million, respectively. The co-effects of  $\\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  contributed to a  $23\\%$  and  $26\\%$  decrease in the number of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths and became one of the major drivers for  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths reduction in the APWBSD phase. This confirms the clear environment and health co-benefits of carbon mitigation measures. Meanwhile, the effectiveness of process and source control measures reduced during 2018 to 2020. With the exhaustion of end-of-pipe and energy consumption control measures, the future air clean actions should focus more on carbon abatement and energy structure optimization, especially under the background of global climate change mitigation. In addition to mitigation in industrial and energy sectors, it is also critical to explore more cost-effective approaches (e.g., agricultural ammonia mitigation) (Zhou et al., 2021; Gu et al., 2021) for pollution control in other sectors.", "Our analysis of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  attributed deaths in China was compared with several recent studies (Table S3). Great progresses was made in previous studies (Zhang et al., 2019a; Li et al., 2019; Ding et al., 2019; McDuffie et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2017; Zou et al., 2019; Apte et al., 2015), albeit with several limitations in common: (1) previous studies focused mainly on the APPCAP (2013-2017) and lack the updated results of the APWBSD (2018-2020); (2) the chemical transport model was used widely in previous research, which showed advantages for predicting the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations without ground measurement, but also has limitations in terms of prediction accuracy (Ma et al., 2022); (3) the Integrated Exposure Response (IER) model used for premature death estimation in previous studies lacked sufficient information on deaths at high  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure, and failed to separate exposure from ambient  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and secondhand smoke, which could potentially introduce uncertainties (Burnett et al., 2014). Our research provides several advances compared to previous studies. First, we quantified the co-benefit of the Air Clean Plan and carbon mitigation measures in China for the environment and health from 2014 to 2020. Second, we used state-of-the-art  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  products with the highest accuracy (Wang et al., 2021; 2022) (Fig. S3 and Fig. S4 for detail) and the finest resolution (spatial resolution of  $1\\mathrm{km}$ , Fig. S2) at the national scale to offer a full-coverage estimation of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths. With these innovations, we not only provided a more accurate estimation of the risk due to exposure to  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , but also to analyze the great spatially heterogeneity of policy effectiveness. Third, we adopted the updated MR-BRT spline method, which fits the risk with a more flexible shape for more reliable estimation of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths (Hystad et al., 2020). The MR-BRT includes more exposure scenarios at high  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  levels during fitting compared with other models such as the IER model (Yusuf et al., 2020; Chowdhury et al., 2022). It also excludes active smoking to control for and reduce potential bias due to this effect (Pope et al., 2018). Finally, our study estimated the co-benefit from  $\\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  to the environment and health under the background of the ACP and NDC pledges for the first time."], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Quantitative Results", "Showcase Prediction Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Geospatial Visualizations", "sub_types": ["Map"]}, "key_entities": ["PM2.5 concentrations", "APPCAP", "APWBSD", "China", "BTH", "YRD"], "concise_summary": "The figure illustrates the average PM2.5 concentrations in China during APPCAP and APWBSD phases, showing regional variations and changes.", "inferred_insight": "The maps depict significant reductions in PM2.5 concentrations across various regions, highlighting the effectiveness of policy interventions during the APPCAP and APWBSD phases.", "paper_id": 20}

content.title: Fig. 3. The proportion of people exposed to  PM_{2.5}  in BTH, PRD, YRD, FWP region and the whole nation at different years (i.e., 2014, 2017, and 2020). The dot vertical lines denote the air quality targets of WHO (i.e., IT-1, IT-2, IT-3, IT-4 and AQG of annual mean concentrations of 35, 25, 15, 10, and  5\mu gm^{-3} ).
content.summary: The figure illustrates the proportion of populations in different regions of China exposed to PM2.5 levels compared to WHO air quality targets for 2014, 2017, and 2020.
content.insight: Significant reduction in populations exposed to high PM2.5 levels, with BTH showing most improvement, yet most still exceeded WHO IT-3 in 2020, indicating ongoing public health challenges.

content.title: Fig. 6. Emission factors of 21 inorganic elements in  PM_{2.5} .
content.summary: Figure 6 illustrates the emission factors of 21 inorganic elements in PM2.5 from gasoline and diesel vehicles under China III, IV, and V standards.
content.insight: The figure shows higher emission factors for Na, Mg, K, and Ca, indicating significant contributions from fuel additives, with lower emissions for other elements from engine wear and catalytic particles.

metadata.full_json:
{"id": "img_173", "source_md": "On-road emissions of fine particles and associated.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\On-road emissions of fine particles and associated\figure_6.jpg", "title": "Fig. 6. Emission factors of 21 inorganic elements in  PM_{2.5} .", "references": ["It could be found out from Fig. 6 that the emission factors of Na, Mg, K, and Ca were obviously larger than those of other inorganic elements for both gasoline and diesel vehicles. This mainly because the fuel was prepared with additives which contained Na, Mg, K, while Ca. Fe, Zn, Sr, Cr, Mn, Pb and some other inorganic elements had lower emission factors, because these mainly came from a small amount of additives, catalysts and partly from the wear during engine operation. The percentages of inorganic elements were less than  $1.0\%$  both in gasoline and diesel vehicles, which was much smaller than  $27.73\%$  that reported by", "Vehicle emission is an important contributor to urban air pollution with the increasing number of motor vehicles. Ten typical vehicles were selected in Wuhan to study the emissions of fine particular matters  $\mathrm{(PM_{2.5})}$  and associated chemical components by on-road tests through a Portable Emission Monitoring System (PEMS). The emission factors of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and the compositions of it from different types of vehicle were obtained. Results showed that the average emission factors of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline and diesel vehicles were 1.266 and  $16.589\mathrm{mg}/$  km. As the emission standard of vehicles increased from China III to China V,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor gradually decreased from 17.385 to  $1.520~\mathrm{mg / km}$ . Emission rate of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  was  $0.0384~\mathrm{mg / s}$  under low speed, and it increased to 0.0775 and  $0.0964~\mathrm{mg / s}$  under the medium and high speeds. The ratio of organic carbon versus elemental carbon (OC/EC) in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline vehicles was 6.89, which was greater than that of diesel vehicles as 3.12. Because gasoline was made of small molecules and the compression ratio of gasoline engine was relatively low, some OC remained in the area where the ignition failed in the cylinder. The top four water-soluble ions with high emission factors were  $\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_4^{2 - }$ ,  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2 + }$ , and  $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ , while K, Na, Ca and Mg had a larger emission factors in the 21 tested inorganic elements. These water-soluble ions and inorganic elements mainly came from the oil burning, fuel additives and engines wear. Results of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission characteristics would help to improve the air quality in Wuhan.", "from vehicles became outstanding in Wuhan. In order to find out the characteristics of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and associated chemical components from on-road emissions in urban areas in Wuhan, which was located in the central and southern China and the climates were high temperature and humidity, ten typical motor vehicles were selected to carry out this research.  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors and emission concentration characteristics of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from motor vehicles under different emission standards at different operating speeds were performed. The characteristics of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  components, including carbonaceous components, water-soluble ions and inorganic elements, were detected as well.  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  composition spectrum of vehicle emission in Wuhan would be finally obtained. Results of this study would help formulating measures for the reduction of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions from vehicles in Wuhan, and provide a reference for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution control of motor vehicles in similar types.", "# 3.1. Emission factors and rates of  $PM_{2,5}$", "# 3.4. Emission characteristics of inorganic elements in  $PM_{2.5}$", "The  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from 10 typical vehicles were analyzed by ICP-MS, and 21 inorganic elements including Na, Mg, K, Ca, V, Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, Zn, Ga, As, Rb, Sr, Ag, Cd, Cs, Ba and Pb were detected. The emission factors of inorganic elements under different emission standards for gasoline vehicles were shown in Fig. 6a and diesel vehicles were shown in Fig. 6b.", "It could be seen from Fig. 6a that the order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China III gasoline vehicles was  $\mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Cu}$ . Their emission factors were  $2.051\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.922\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.827\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.783\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.142\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.048\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.026\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.011\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.009\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China IV gasoline vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb}$ . Their emission factors were  $3.423\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.740\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.272\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.534\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.136\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.100\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.059\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.034\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.022\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ . The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China V gasoline vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb}$ . Their emission factors were  $1.047\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.852\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.689\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.202\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.065\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.027\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.026\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.015\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.005\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ .", "The emission factors of all the tested inorganic elements from diesel vehicles were larger than gasoline vehicles when compared Fig. 6b with Fig. 6a, which was consistent with the fact that  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of diesel vehicles was larger than gasoline vehicles. The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China III diesel vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb}$ . Their emission factors were  $100.10\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $30.65\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $19.26\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $6.84\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $3.72\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $2.05\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.83\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.75\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.64\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China IV diesel vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb}$ , which was same as China III. Their emission factors were  $81.00\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $28.73\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $16.80\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $4.06\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $3.96\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.62\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.08\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.71\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.69\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The order of inorganic elements with the top eight emission factors for China V diesel vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb} > \mathrm{Zn}$ . Their emission factors were  $74.12\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $7.85\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $7.32\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $2.36\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.20\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.53\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.40\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.29\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively.", "Laboratory analysis on collected filter membranes was carried out to study the characteristics of carbonaceous components, water-soluble ions and inorganic elements. Emission factor of carbonaceous components emitted from diesel vehicles was 9.15 times higher than that of gasoline vehicles. However, the OC/EC ratio of gasoline vehicles was over twice of diesel vehicles. The compression ratio of gasoline engine was relatively low, and some OC remained in the area where the ignition failed in the cylinder.", "The water-soluble ions with the top four emission factors was  $\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}$ ,  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$  and  $\mathrm{Na}^+$ . Their percentages in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  increased slightly with high humidity and the order of top water-soluble ions kept the same. Their emission factors of diesel vehicles were all larger than gasoline vehicles. The highest emission factors of inorganic elements in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  were K, Ca, Na and Mg, while Fe, Zn, Sr, Cr, Mn, Pb and other inorganic elements had lower emission factors.  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  composition spectrum of vehicle emission in Wuhan was obtained and the largest proportion was OC. content.title: Fig. 2. Emission factors of  PM_2.5 emitted from 10 tested vehicles.
content.summary: The figure displays PM2.5 emission factors for gasoline and diesel vehicles under different China emission standards (III, IV, V).
content.insight: As emission standards improve, PM2.5 emission factors decrease. Diesel vehicles emit higher PM2.5 levels than gasoline vehicles under the same standard.
