metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_170", "source_md": "On-road emissions of fine particles and associated.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\On-road emissions of fine particles and associated\\figure_2.jpg", "title": "Fig. 2. Emission factors of  PM_2.5 emitted from 10 tested vehicles.", "references": ["The  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions of six gasoline vehicles were shown in Fig. 2. There was only one China III gasoline vehicle and its  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor was  $1.675\\mathrm{mg / km}$ . Three gasoline vehicles with China IV emission standard had an average  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of  $1.452\\mathrm{mg / km}$ . The average  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of other two China V gasoline vehicles was  $0.783\\mathrm{mg / km}$ . The  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors of gasoline vehicles decreased gradually with the increasing emission standards. Four diesel vehicles were tested in this experiment as well. Only one China III diesel vehicle was tested and its  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor was  $33.095\\mathrm{mg / km}$ . Two diesel vehicles with China IV emission standard had an average  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of  $15.134\\mathrm{mg / km}$ . There was only one China V diesel vehicle and its  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor was  $2.994\\mathrm{mg / km}$ . As the emission standards rose up from China III to China V, the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of diesel vehicles decreased by more than  $90\\%$ . As emission standards became stricter, the emission factor of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  gradually decreased because vehicles with higher emission standards usually had better exhaust control devices. Meanwhile, vehicles with higher emission standards were often younger than vehicles with lower emission standards (Zhang et al., 2019a, b). Under the same emission standards, the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors of diesel vehicles were obviously higher than gasoline vehicles, because the molecules that made up diesel were usually larger than gasoline (Distillation range of gasoline and diesel see Table s5.), and the engines could not be evenly mixed with air during", "Vehicle emission is an important contributor to urban air pollution with the increasing number of motor vehicles. Ten typical vehicles were selected in Wuhan to study the emissions of fine particular matters  $\\mathrm{(PM_{2.5})}$  and associated chemical components by on-road tests through a Portable Emission Monitoring System (PEMS). The emission factors of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and the compositions of it from different types of vehicle were obtained. Results showed that the average emission factors of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline and diesel vehicles were 1.266 and  $16.589\\mathrm{mg}/$  km. As the emission standard of vehicles increased from China III to China V,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor gradually decreased from 17.385 to  $1.520~\\mathrm{mg / km}$ . Emission rate of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  was  $0.0384~\\mathrm{mg / s}$  under low speed, and it increased to 0.0775 and  $0.0964~\\mathrm{mg / s}$  under the medium and high speeds. The ratio of organic carbon versus elemental carbon (OC/EC) in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline vehicles was 6.89, which was greater than that of diesel vehicles as 3.12. Because gasoline was made of small molecules and the compression ratio of gasoline engine was relatively low, some OC remained in the area where the ignition failed in the cylinder. The top four water-soluble ions with high emission factors were  $\\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_4^{2 - }$ ,  $\\mathrm{Ca}^{2 + }$ , and  $\\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ , while K, Na, Ca and Mg had a larger emission factors in the 21 tested inorganic elements. These water-soluble ions and inorganic elements mainly came from the oil burning, fuel additives and engines wear. Results of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission characteristics would help to improve the air quality in Wuhan.", "As the pollution of vehicle emission becomes a prominent social problem, the monitoring methods of vehicle emissions are developed and improved continuously (Giechaskiel et al., 2014; Xue et al., 2018; Farwick et al., 2019). These methods mainly include remote sensing detection, vehicle bench test, tunnel test and portable vehicle emission measurement (Giechaskiel et al., 2018; Mahesh et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2020a, b). Remote sensing is an effective and economic tool to monitor vehicle emissions, while it is accurate in identifying gasoline vehicles but not diesel ones (Huang et al., 2018). And the chemical composition of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  can not be obtained by remote sensing (Smit et al., 2019). Myung et al. (2009) carried out bench tests on light motor vehicles and found that  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions of using liquefied petroleum gas and biodiesel were much lower than those of gasoline and diesel. Carbonaceous components in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from vehicles could be studied through a bench test, and it found that the driving speed directly affected the EC emissions (Fraser et al., 2002). However, the fluence of actual driving conditions on vehicle emissions could not be reflected (Pouresmaeili et al., 2018). Sánchez et al. (2019) studied the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors of both light and heavy vehicles through tunnel experiments, and the emission factors of heavy vehicles tended to be much higher than those of light vehicles. Tunnel experiments were also conducted by Hao et al. (2019) to study their characteristics of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions from motor vehicles in Xi'an, China, and the results showed that OC accounted for  $34.10\\%$  of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ . Since tunnels in different locations have different speed limits, and it is difficult to cover both high-speed and real-world driving conditions in some tunnels (Huang et al., 2017; Smit et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2020a, b). Huang et al. (2013) used PEMS to detect the concentrations of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions from buses, and cars under actual driving conditions, and they found that concentrations of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  increased with the driving speed. PEMS was also used to analyze the chemical composition of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from heavy diesel trucks, and it was found that EC and OC were the major components of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (Zhang et al., 2015). Based on the results of previous researches, the conclusions drawn from various cities were obviously different due to their geographical locations, climates, populations, etc. Wuhan is located in the central of China, and it has a large population and a large number of motor vehicles with high humidity climate (Yang et al., 2019). The effect of humidity on some gaseous pollutants (such as CO and NOx) emitted from vehicles has been studied (Dolly et al., 2020), while few research were carried out on the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and its chemical components. Therefore, it was necessary to carry out the research on vehicle emissions of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in Wuhan, China.", "from vehicles became outstanding in Wuhan. In order to find out the characteristics of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and associated chemical components from on-road emissions in urban areas in Wuhan, which was located in the central and southern China and the climates were high temperature and humidity, ten typical motor vehicles were selected to carry out this research.  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors and emission concentration characteristics of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from motor vehicles under different emission standards at different operating speeds were performed. The characteristics of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  components, including carbonaceous components, water-soluble ions and inorganic elements, were detected as well.  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  composition spectrum of vehicle emission in Wuhan would be finally obtained. Results of this study would help formulating measures for the reduction of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions from vehicles in Wuhan, and provide a reference for  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution control of motor vehicles in similar types.", "$\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from the tested vehicles were collected by the SEMTECH-PFS sub-module. SEMTECH-PFS sub-module had three channels for membrane sampling, where three sampling membranes with a diameter of  $47\\mathrm{mm}$  were located. These three channels could not be used to collect  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  at the same time. When one channel was ventilated and sampling, the other two channels were closed. Computer was used to decide the channel of the airflow. Before sampling started, a quartz filter (MK360, Munktel, Sweden) was placed on Channel 1, a Teflon filter (R2PJ047, PALL, USA) was placed on Channel 2 and another quartz filter was placed on Chanel 3. The quartz filter membrane on Chanel 1 was mainly used for analyzing organic carbon, elemental carbon and water-soluble ions. The Teflon filter membrane on Chanel 2 was used for analyzing elements. The quartz filter membrane on Chanel 3 was used as the blank sample.", "Carbonaceous aerosols consisted of organic carbon (OC), elemental carbon (EC), and a small amount of carbonate. Since carbonates were very small part in atmospheric particles and the proportion of carbonates was less than  $5\\%$  of total carbon (TC), they would not be discussed in this study. The DRI thermal carbon analyzer (IMPROVE-A, DRI, USA) was used to detect the concentration of OC and EC, when  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  sample was collected without the pretreatment after equilibrium weighing. Several water-soluble ions in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from the tested vehicles were analyzed, which included  $\\mathrm{K}^{+}$ ,  $\\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$ ,  $\\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ ,  $\\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ ,  $\\mathrm{NH}_4^+$ ,  $\\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_3^-$  and  $\\mathrm{F}^-$ . Ion chromatograph (IC) was used to analyze the water-soluble ions that collected on the filter. 21 crustal elements including Na, Mg, K, Ca, V, Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, Zn, Ga, As, Rb, Sr, Ag, Cd, Cs, Ba and Pb were detected by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS). Microwave digester was used as the pretreatment of collected filter samples. In order to find out the content of crustal elements in the", "The mass concentration of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from vehicle emissions could be obtained through the CPM sub-module in SEMTECH-ECOSTAR. Combining the exhaust gas flow data recorded by the exhaust gas flow meter and the vehicle driving conditions data recorded by the GPS, the emission rate under the actual driving conditions could be calculated. According to the driving speed, the driving conditions of the tested vehicles were divided into eight speed segments: idle speed (0 km/h), 0–10 km/h, 0–20 km/h, 20–30 km/h, 30–40 km/h, 40–50 km/h, 50–60 km/h and >60 km/h. The average  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rates of the tested vehicles in all the eight speed segments were calculated, and the results were shown in Fig. 3.", "Table 2 Emission factors of carbonaceous components in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from vehicles.", "In Table 2, the average emission factors of TC for China III, IV and V gasoline vehicles were 1086.15, 1054.06 and  $397.94~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. When compared to China III vehicles, the TC emission factors of China IV and V vehicles decreased by  $2.95\\%$  and  $63.36\\%$ . Meanwhile, it could be found that the OC emission factors of the tested gasoline vehicles were 8.68 times larger than those of EC except Vehicle 1#. TC emission factor of diesel vehicles was 9.15 times higher than that of gasoline vehicles. To find out the TC emission characteristics of diesel vehicles with different emission standards, the average emission factors of TC for China III, IV and V vehicles were 25,973.93, 11,745.64 and  $1735.88~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. When compared to China III diesel vehicles, the TC emission factors of China IV and V vehicles decreased by  $54.78\\%$  and  $93.32\\%$ . OC emission factors of diesel vehicles were also 1.06 times larger than EC emission factors, which was similar to the OC and EC emission characteristics of gasoline vehicles.", "The ratio of OC/EC was an important parameter in the carbonaceous components of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , which was often used to determine the source of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ . The OC/EC ratios of carbonaceous compositions from the 10 tested vehicles were shown in Fig. 4, and the average OC/EC ratios of the vehicles with different emission standards were figured out as well. The OC/EC ratio of gasoline vehicles was higher than 3.0 except for Vehicle 1#. It meant there were many organic components in the carbonaceous component, which was consistent with the results of some recent studies (James et al., 2002). The OC/EC ratio of Vehicle 1# was only 0.3, which deviated from the normal value of gasoline vehicle emissions. Because Vehicle 1# was a taxi with both gasoline and natural gas functions, and the engine used a mixture of gasoline and natural gas for a long time. It used gasoline fuel during the test, and insufficient combustion of gasoline in the engine caused an increase in the number of black carbon particles, which led to a small ratio of OC/EC. The average OC/EC ratio of China IV Vehicles was 10.2, and it decreased to 5.3 in China V gasoline vehicles. This indicated the improvement of emission standards could reduce the percentage of OC in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from gasoline vehicles. For diesel vehicles, the average OC/EC ratios of China III, IV and V vehicles were 1.5, 3.0, and 4.9, respectively. The OC/EC ratio of diesel vehicles turned to decrease as the emission standard rose up. It could also be seen from Fig. 4 that the OC/EC ratio of gasoline vehicles was generally greater than that of diesel vehicles, which was mainly because diesel was more likely to form unburned soot particles during the combustion process (Shen et al., 2021). There were two main differences between gasoline vehicles and diesel vehicles, fuel composition and engine structure. Gasoline was made of small molecules and was easy to mix evenly with air. After the spark plug discharged ignition, the fuel burned rapidly. The compression ratio of the gasoline engine was relatively low, and a large amount of OC remained in the area where the", "The order of the water-soluble ion emission factors in the China III gasoline vehicles was  $\\mathrm{Cl}^{-} > \\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - } > \\mathrm{Ca}^{2 + } > \\mathrm{Na}^{+} > \\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-} > \\mathrm{Mg}^{2 + } > \\mathrm{K}^{+}>$ $\\mathrm{F}^{-} > \\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}$  as shown in Fig. 5a. The emission factors of  $\\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - }$  and  $\\mathrm{Ca^{2 + }}$  in the top three positions were  $123.50~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $73.51~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$  and  $62.65~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The top three emission factors of the water-soluble ions in China IV gasoline vehicles were  $\\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ ,  $\\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ and  $\\mathrm{Ca^{2 + }}$ , which were  $56.71~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $26.84~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$  and  $20.12~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The top three water-soluble ions in China V gasoline vehicles were  $\\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - }$  and  $\\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ , and their emission factors were  $52.28~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $30.91~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$  and  $21.70~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. With the exception of  $\\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - }$ , the emission factors of the tested water-soluble ions decreased gradually as the emission standard increased, which was consistent with the trend of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  changing with emission standards. As could be seen from Fig. 5b that the order of the water-soluble ions emission factors in China III diesel vehicles was  $\\mathrm{Cl}^{-} > \\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - } > \\mathrm{Mg}^{2 + } > \\mathrm{Na}^{+} > \\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-} > \\mathrm{Ca}^{2 + } > \\mathrm{K}^{+}>$ $\\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+} > \\mathrm{F}^{-}$ . The average emission factors of  $\\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - }$ , and  $\\mathrm{Mg}^{2 + }$  in the top three were  $1194.27~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $514.26~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$  and  $359.95~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ . The top three emission factors of the water-soluble ions in China IV diesel vehicles were  $\\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - }$  and  $\\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ , which were  $844.52~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $487.50~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$  and  $456.73~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The top three water-soluble ions in China V diesel vehicles were  $\\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - }$  and  $\\mathrm{Mg}^{2 + }$ , and their emission factors were  $167.63~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $110.68~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$  and  $71.82~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively.", "From the perspective of changes in emission standards, the emission factors of  $\\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}$  and  $\\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$  decreased as the emission standards increased. While the emission factors of  $\\mathrm{NH}_4^+$ ,  $\\mathrm{F}^-$ ,  $\\mathrm{Na}^+$ ,  $\\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$  and  $\\mathrm{NO}_3^-$  increased to the top in China IV diesel vehicles. The percentages of  $\\mathrm{Na}^+$ ,  $\\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$ ,  $\\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}$  and  $\\mathrm{NO}_3^-$  in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline vehicles were  $2.258\\%$ ,  $1.788\\%$ ,  $0.773\\%$ ,  $2.186\\%$  and  $0.623\\%$ , respectively. While their percentages from diesel vehicles were  $2.683\\%$ ,  $0.789\\%$ ,  $1.782\\%$ ,  $2.918\\%$  and  $0.857\\%$ , respectively. The percentage of water-soluble ions in this study (13.12% in gasoline vehicles and 14.53% in diesel vehicles) were higher than  $10\\%$  reported by Zhang et al. (2015), whose research was conducted in Beijing, a relatively dry city. However, the kinds of top water-soluble ions and the orders of them were similar to this study. Thus, humidity helped increase the percentage of water-soluble ions in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and still keep the order of top water-soluble ions. Cheng et al. (2010) studied the on-road  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in Hong Kong, a coastal city with higher humidity than Wuhan. They found the percentage of", "The  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from 10 typical vehicles were analyzed by ICP-MS, and 21 inorganic elements including Na, Mg, K, Ca, V, Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, Zn, Ga, As, Rb, Sr, Ag, Cd, Cs, Ba and Pb were detected. The emission factors of inorganic elements under different emission standards for gasoline vehicles were shown in Fig. 6a and diesel vehicles were shown in Fig. 6b.", "It could be seen from Fig. 6a that the order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China III gasoline vehicles was  $\\mathrm{Na} > \\mathrm{Ca} > \\mathrm{K} > \\mathrm{Mg} > \\mathrm{Fe} > \\mathrm{Zn} > \\mathrm{Cr} > \\mathrm{Sr} > \\mathrm{Cu}$ . Their emission factors were  $2.051\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.922\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.827\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.783\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.142\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.048\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.026\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.011\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.009\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China IV gasoline vehicles was  $\\mathrm{K} > \\mathrm{Ca} > \\mathrm{Na} > \\mathrm{Mg} > \\mathrm{Sr} > \\mathrm{Fe} > \\mathrm{Zn} > \\mathrm{Cr} > \\mathrm{Pb}$ . Their emission factors were  $3.423\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.740\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.272\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.534\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.136\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.100\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.059\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.034\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.022\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ . The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China V gasoline vehicles was  $\\mathrm{K} > \\mathrm{Ca} > \\mathrm{Na} > \\mathrm{Mg} > \\mathrm{Fe} > \\mathrm{Sr} > \\mathrm{Zn} > \\mathrm{Cr} > \\mathrm{Pb}$ . Their emission factors were  $1.047\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.852\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.689\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.202\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.065\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.027\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.026\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.015\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.005\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ .", "The emission factors of all the tested inorganic elements from diesel vehicles were larger than gasoline vehicles when compared Fig. 6b with Fig. 6a, which was consistent with the fact that  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of diesel vehicles was larger than gasoline vehicles. The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China III diesel vehicles was  $\\mathrm{K} > \\mathrm{Ca} > \\mathrm{Na} > \\mathrm{Mg} > \\mathrm{Sr} > \\mathrm{Fe} > \\mathrm{Zn} > \\mathrm{Cr} > \\mathrm{Pb}$ . Their emission factors were  $100.10\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $30.65\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $19.26\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $6.84\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $3.72\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $2.05\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.83\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.75\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.64\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China IV diesel vehicles was  $\\mathrm{K} > \\mathrm{Ca} > \\mathrm{Na} > \\mathrm{Mg} > \\mathrm{Sr} > \\mathrm{Fe} > \\mathrm{Zn} > \\mathrm{Cr} > \\mathrm{Pb}$ , which was same as China III. Their emission factors were  $81.00\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $28.73\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $16.80\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $4.06\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $3.96\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.62\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.08\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.71\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.69\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The order of inorganic elements with the top eight emission factors for China V diesel vehicles was  $\\mathrm{K} > \\mathrm{Na} > \\mathrm{Ca} > \\mathrm{Sr} > \\mathrm{Fe} > \\mathrm{Cr} > \\mathrm{Pb} > \\mathrm{Zn}$ . Their emission factors were  $74.12\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $7.85\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $7.32\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $2.36\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.20\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.53\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.40\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.29\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively.", "It could be found out from Fig. 6 that the emission factors of Na, Mg, K, and Ca were obviously larger than those of other inorganic elements for both gasoline and diesel vehicles. This mainly because the fuel was prepared with additives which contained Na, Mg, K, while Ca. Fe, Zn, Sr, Cr, Mn, Pb and some other inorganic elements had lower emission factors, because these mainly came from a small amount of additives, catalysts and partly from the wear during engine operation. The percentages of inorganic elements were less than  $1.0\\%$  both in gasoline and diesel vehicles, which was much smaller than  $27.73\\%$  that reported by", "The data of Table 3 showed that OC and EC were the two types of substances that accounted for the largest proportion of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in vehicle emissions. The proportion of OC in gasoline and diesel vehicles were  $53.926\\%$  and  $51.151\\%$ , respectively. The proportion of EC in diesel vehicles (25.169%) was nearly twice as much as gasoline vehicles (13.068%), which indicated more EC components were emitted from diesel vehicles than gasoline vehicles.  $\\mathrm{Cl}^{-}, \\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}, \\mathrm{Na}^{+}, \\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$  and  $\\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$  were the water-soluble ions with the top five proportions in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from the tested vehicles. The proportion of  $\\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$  in gasoline vehicles was more than twice of diesel vehicles, while the proportion of  $\\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$  in diesel vehicles was more than twice of gasoline vehicles. These might be because the additives in gasoline and diesel vehicles were different. The additives in gasoline vehicles might contain more Ca, while additives in diesel vehicles might contain more Mg. K, Na, Ca, Mg, Sr, Fe, Zn and Pb were the inorganic elements with higher proportions in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from the tested vehicles. K, Na, Ca, and Mg were mainly derived from fuel additives, while Sr, Fe, Zn and Pb were mainly derived from the wear and tear of vehicles engines.", "Laboratory analysis on collected filter membranes was carried out to study the characteristics of carbonaceous components, water-soluble ions and inorganic elements. Emission factor of carbonaceous components emitted from diesel vehicles was 9.15 times higher than that of gasoline vehicles. However, the OC/EC ratio of gasoline vehicles was over twice of diesel vehicles. The compression ratio of gasoline engine was relatively low, and some OC remained in the area where the ignition failed in the cylinder.", "Cui, M., Chen, Y.J., Tian, C.G., Zhang, F., Yan, C.Q., Zheng, M., 2016. content.title: Fig. 6. (a) Trends in observed annual  PM_{2.5}  concentrations for BTH and 74 key cities between 2013 and 2017; (b) trends expressed as 90th percentiles in maximum daily average  8hO_3  concentration. Shaded areas represent the 25th-75th percentile range. Data are available in Table S1 in Appendix A. Details on monthly  PM_{2.5}  concentration for 74 key cities are summarized in Tables S2-S6.  PM_{2.5}  and  O_3  concentrations in the BTH region and 74 cities were obtained from the China National Environmental Monitoring Center.
content.summary: The figure shows trends in PM2.5 and O3 concentrations between 2013 and 2017 in BTH and 74 key cities, highlighting a decrease in PM2.5 and an increase in O3 levels.
content.insight: Despite reductions in PM2.5, O3 pollution increased, posing challenges in simultaneous mitigation and highlighting the need for integrated pollution control strategies in China.

metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_180", "source_md": "Progress of Air Pollution Control in China and Its.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Progress of Air Pollution Control in China and Its\\figure_6.jpg", "title": "Fig. 6. (a) Trends in observed annual  PM_{2.5}  concentrations for BTH and 74 key cities between 2013 and 2017; (b) trends expressed as 90th percentiles in maximum daily average  8hO_3  concentration. Shaded areas represent the 25th-75th percentile range. Data are available in Table S1 in Appendix A. Details on monthly  PM_{2.5}  concentration for 74 key cities are summarized in Tables S2-S6.  PM_{2.5}  and  O_3  concentrations in the BTH region and 74 cities were obtained from the China National Environmental Monitoring Center.", "references": ["Despite the substantial progress that has been made in addressing acid rain and  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  issues (Fig. 6(a)), an unwanted increase in ground-level  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  pollution is still found in many regions across China, posing the more complex challenge of simultaneous continuous mitigation of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution with an emerging  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  issue. As  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  pollution has been steadily increasing in most parts of eastern China [47-50],  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  issues have recently gained more attention among both the public and academia in China. As illustrated in Fig. 6(b), the average 90th percentiles in daily maximum  $8\\mathrm{h}$ $\\mathrm{O}_3$  concentrations increased by  $20.1\\%$  and  $24.5\\%$  in 74 key cities and BTH, respectively, from 2013 to 2017. Furthermore, densely populated regions of eastern China were exposed to high  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  pollution, increasing the impacts of  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  on human health. The results of the GBD project [51] estimated that respiratory mortality attributable to long-term  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  exposure in adults is between 274000 and 316000 in China, equal to approximately  $25\\% -29\\%$  of the deaths attributable to  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure [16]. It is necessary to integrate  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  and  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  controls to ensure a pollution-free and healthy living environment in the development of ecological civilization in China.", "The biggest challenge of  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  pollution control is rooted in the complexity of atmospheric photochemistry formation, which depends on  $\\mathrm{O_3 - NO_x - VOC}$  sensitivity diagnosis and meteorological conditions [52,53]. A control strategy designed to reduce emissions of  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  or VOCs for  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  control may result in an unexpected disadvantage for  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  reduction. Strong VOC-limiting systems are found in most urban areas of China [54,55], and  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  in urban areas can be enhanced if few or no VOCs are controlled simultaneously with  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  [56]. For example, during the Action Plan from 2013 to 2017, anthropogenic emissions of  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  were reduced by  $21\\%$  but VOCs increased by  $2\\%$  nationwide [57]. Such combinational changes in  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ -VOC emissions might be beneficial for reducing  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  but not for  $\\mathrm{O_3}$ , as shown by the decreased  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and increased  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  concentrations during the same period (Fig. 6). It is notable that the increase in  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  concentration during the Action Plan period is also attributable to variation in meteorological conditions—specifically, changing temperature and short-wave radiation. Therefore, full consideration of meteorology and optimal mitigation of precursor emissions are critical in designing an effective  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  control strategy."], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Present Quantitative Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Line Chart"]}, "key_entities": ["PM2.5", "O3", "BTH region", "China National Environmental Monitoring Center", "90th percentiles"], "concise_summary": "The figure shows trends in PM2.5 and O3 concentrations between 2013 and 2017 in BTH and 74 key cities, highlighting a decrease in PM2.5 and an increase in O3 levels.", "inferred_insight": "Despite reductions in PM2.5, O3 pollution increased, posing challenges in simultaneous mitigation and highlighting the need for integrated pollution control strategies in China.", "paper_id": 26}

content.title: Fig. 11. (a)-(d): comparison of the predicted and measured emission factors for the chemical constituents of PM. The measured data has been extracted from Agrawal et al. (2008a), Moldanova et al. (2009), Petzold et al. (2008) and Murphy et al. (2009).
content.summary: The figure compares predicted and measured emission factors of PM's chemical constituents from different studies, showcasing performance variations against the STEAM2 model.
content.insight: The comparison highlights discrepancies between predictions by the STEAM2 model and measurements from various studies, emphasizing model calibration needs.

content.title: Fig. 7. Percentages of different categories in  PM_{2.5}  emitted from vehicles.
content.summary: The figure shows the percentages of PM2.5 components—organic carbon, elemental carbon, water-soluble ions, and inorganic elements—emitted from gasoline and diesel vehicles.
content.insight: The figure highlights the dominance of organic carbon in PM2.5 emissions and a significant difference in elemental carbon between gasoline and diesel vehicles, important for understanding vehicle pollution.

metadata.full_json:
{"id": "img_174", "source_md": "On-road emissions of fine particles and associated.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\On-road emissions of fine particles and associated\figure_7.jpg", "title": "Fig. 7. Percentages of different categories in  PM_{2.5}  emitted from vehicles.", "references": ["The components in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  could be divided into five categories: OC, EC, water-soluble ions, inorganic elements and other components. Each percentages of these five categories in the particulate matter were calculated, and the results were shown in Fig. 7. The proportions of OC, EC, water-soluble ions and inorganic elements in gasoline vehicles were  $53.93\%$ $13.07\%$ $13.12\%$  and  $0.30\%$  respectively. The proportions of OC, EC, water-soluble ions and inorganic elements in diesel vehicles were  $51.15\%$ $25.17\%$ $14.53\%$  and  $0.44\%$  respectively. Thus, the largest difference in the proportion of various components in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from gasoline and diesel vehicles was EC.", "Vehicle emission is an important contributor to urban air pollution with the increasing number of motor vehicles. Ten typical vehicles were selected in Wuhan to study the emissions of fine particular matters  $\mathrm{(PM_{2.5})}$  and associated chemical components by on-road tests through a Portable Emission Monitoring System (PEMS). The emission factors of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and the compositions of it from different types of vehicle were obtained. Results showed that the average emission factors of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline and diesel vehicles were 1.266 and  $16.589\mathrm{mg}/$  km. As the emission standard of vehicles increased from China III to China V,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor gradually decreased from 17.385 to  $1.520~\mathrm{mg / km}$ . Emission rate of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  was  $0.0384~\mathrm{mg / s}$  under low speed, and it increased to 0.0775 and  $0.0964~\mathrm{mg / s}$  under the medium and high speeds. The ratio of organic carbon versus elemental carbon (OC/EC) in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline vehicles was 6.89, which was greater than that of diesel vehicles as 3.12. Because gasoline was made of small molecules and the compression ratio of gasoline engine was relatively low, some OC remained in the area where the ignition failed in the cylinder. The top four water-soluble ions with high emission factors were  $\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_4^{2 - }$ ,  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2 + }$ , and  $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ , while K, Na, Ca and Mg had a larger emission factors in the 21 tested inorganic elements. These water-soluble ions and inorganic elements mainly came from the oil burning, fuel additives and engines wear. Results of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission characteristics would help to improve the air quality in Wuhan.", "As the pollution of vehicle emission becomes a prominent social problem, the monitoring methods of vehicle emissions are developed and improved continuously (Giechaskiel et al., 2014; Xue et al., 2018; Farwick et al., 2019). These methods mainly include remote sensing detection, vehicle bench test, tunnel test and portable vehicle emission measurement (Giechaskiel et al., 2018; Mahesh et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2020a, b). Remote sensing is an effective and economic tool to monitor vehicle emissions, while it is accurate in identifying gasoline vehicles but not diesel ones (Huang et al., 2018). And the chemical composition of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  can not be obtained by remote sensing (Smit et al., 2019). Myung et al. (2009) carried out bench tests on light motor vehicles and found that  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions of using liquefied petroleum gas and biodiesel were much lower than those of gasoline and diesel. Carbonaceous components in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from vehicles could be studied through a bench test, and it found that the driving speed directly affected the EC emissions (Fraser et al., 2002). However, the fluence of actual driving conditions on vehicle emissions could not be reflected (Pouresmaeili et al., 2018). Sánchez et al. (2019) studied the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors of both light and heavy vehicles through tunnel experiments, and the emission factors of heavy vehicles tended to be much higher than those of light vehicles. Tunnel experiments were also conducted by Hao et al. (2019) to study their characteristics of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions from motor vehicles in Xi'an, China, and the results showed that OC accounted for  $34.10\%$  of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ . Since tunnels in different locations have different speed limits, and it is difficult to cover both high-speed and real-world driving conditions in some tunnels (Huang et al., 2017; Smit et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2020a, b). Huang et al. (2013) used PEMS to detect the concentrations of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions from buses, and cars under actual driving conditions, and they found that concentrations of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  increased with the driving speed. PEMS was also used to analyze the chemical composition of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from heavy diesel trucks, and it was found that EC and OC were the major components of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (Zhang et al., 2015). Based on the results of previous researches, the conclusions drawn from various cities were obviously different due to their geographical locations, climates, populations, etc. Wuhan is located in the central of China, and it has a large population and a large number of motor vehicles with high humidity climate (Yang et al., 2019). The effect of humidity on some gaseous pollutants (such as CO and NOx) emitted from vehicles has been studied (Dolly et al., 2020), while few research were carried out on the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and its chemical components. Therefore, it was necessary to carry out the research on vehicle emissions of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in Wuhan, China.", "The ratio of OC/EC was an important parameter in the carbonaceous components of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , which was often used to determine the source of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ . The OC/EC ratios of carbonaceous compositions from the 10 tested vehicles were shown in Fig. 4, and the average OC/EC ratios of the vehicles with different emission standards were figured out as well. The OC/EC ratio of gasoline vehicles was higher than 3.0 except for Vehicle 1#. It meant there were many organic components in the carbonaceous component, which was consistent with the results of some recent studies (James et al., 2002). The OC/EC ratio of Vehicle 1# was only 0.3, which deviated from the normal value of gasoline vehicle emissions. Because Vehicle 1# was a taxi with both gasoline and natural gas functions, and the engine used a mixture of gasoline and natural gas for a long time. It used gasoline fuel during the test, and insufficient combustion of gasoline in the engine caused an increase in the number of black carbon particles, which led to a small ratio of OC/EC. The average OC/EC ratio of China IV Vehicles was 10.2, and it decreased to 5.3 in China V gasoline vehicles. This indicated the improvement of emission standards could reduce the percentage of OC in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from gasoline vehicles. For diesel vehicles, the average OC/EC ratios of China III, IV and V vehicles were 1.5, 3.0, and 4.9, respectively. The OC/EC ratio of diesel vehicles turned to decrease as the emission standard rose up. It could also be seen from Fig. 4 that the OC/EC ratio of gasoline vehicles was generally greater than that of diesel vehicles, which was mainly because diesel was more likely to form unburned soot particles during the combustion process (Shen et al., 2021). There were two main differences between gasoline vehicles and diesel vehicles, fuel composition and engine structure. Gasoline was made of small molecules and was easy to mix evenly with air. After the spark plug discharged ignition, the fuel burned rapidly. The compression ratio of the gasoline engine was relatively low, and a large amount of OC remained in the area where the", "The  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from 10 typical vehicles were analyzed by ICP-MS, and 21 inorganic elements including Na, Mg, K, Ca, V, Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, Zn, Ga, As, Rb, Sr, Ag, Cd, Cs, Ba and Pb were detected. The emission factors of inorganic elements under different emission standards for gasoline vehicles were shown in Fig. 6a and diesel vehicles were shown in Fig. 6b.", "The data of Table 3 showed that OC and EC were the two types of substances that accounted for the largest proportion of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in vehicle emissions. The proportion of OC in gasoline and diesel vehicles were  $53.926\%$  and  $51.151\%$ , respectively. The proportion of EC in diesel vehicles (25.169%) was nearly twice as much as gasoline vehicles (13.068%), which indicated more EC components were emitted from diesel vehicles than gasoline vehicles.  $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}, \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}, \mathrm{Na}^{+}, \mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$  and  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$  were the water-soluble ions with the top five proportions in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from the tested vehicles. The proportion of  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$  in gasoline vehicles was more than twice of diesel vehicles, while the proportion of  $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$  in diesel vehicles was more than twice of gasoline vehicles. These might be because the additives in gasoline and diesel vehicles were different. The additives in gasoline vehicles might contain more Ca, while additives in diesel vehicles might contain more Mg. K, Na, Ca, Mg, Sr, Fe, Zn and Pb were the inorganic elements with higher proportions in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from the tested vehicles. K, Na, Ca, and Mg were mainly derived from fuel additives, while Sr, Fe, Zn and Pb were mainly derived from the wear and tear of vehicles engines.", "Mukherjee, A., Agrawal, M., 2018. Assessment of local and distant sources of urban PM2.5 in middle Indo-Gangetic plain of India using statistical modeling. Atmos. Res. 213, 275-287. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.atmosres.2018.06.014.

The average emission rate of  PM_2.5  in different speed segments.", "supplement": "average emission rate(mg/s) range of driving speed(km/h)", "references": ["The mass concentration of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from vehicle emissions could be obtained through the CPM sub-module in SEMTECH-ECOSTAR. Combining the exhaust gas flow data recorded by the exhaust gas flow meter and the vehicle driving conditions data recorded by the GPS, the emission rate under the actual driving conditions could be calculated. According to the driving speed, the driving conditions of the tested vehicles were divided into eight speed segments: idle speed (0 km/h), 0–10 km/h, 0–20 km/h, 20–30 km/h, 30–40 km/h, 40–50 km/h, 50–60 km/h and >60 km/h. The average  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rates of the tested vehicles in all the eight speed segments were calculated, and the results were shown in Fig. 3.", "Vehicle emission is an important contributor to urban air pollution with the increasing number of motor vehicles. Ten typical vehicles were selected in Wuhan to study the emissions of fine particular matters  $\\mathrm{(PM_{2.5})}$  and associated chemical components by on-road tests through a Portable Emission Monitoring System (PEMS). The emission factors of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and the compositions of it from different types of vehicle were obtained. Results showed that the average emission factors of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline and diesel vehicles were 1.266 and  $16.589\\mathrm{mg}/$  km. As the emission standard of vehicles increased from China III to China V,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor gradually decreased from 17.385 to  $1.520~\\mathrm{mg / km}$ . Emission rate of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  was  $0.0384~\\mathrm{mg / s}$  under low speed, and it increased to 0.0775 and  $0.0964~\\mathrm{mg / s}$  under the medium and high speeds. The ratio of organic carbon versus elemental carbon (OC/EC) in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline vehicles was 6.89, which was greater than that of diesel vehicles as 3.12. Because gasoline was made of small molecules and the compression ratio of gasoline engine was relatively low, some OC remained in the area where the ignition failed in the cylinder. The top four water-soluble ions with high emission factors were  $\\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_4^{2 - }$ ,  $\\mathrm{Ca}^{2 + }$ , and  $\\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ , while K, Na, Ca and Mg had a larger emission factors in the 21 tested inorganic elements. These water-soluble ions and inorganic elements mainly came from the oil burning, fuel additives and engines wear. Results of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission characteristics would help to improve the air quality in Wuhan.", "A portable vehicle on-board test system was used in this experiment to detect the vehicle emissions and collected  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  samples, and the schematic diagram of PEMS was shown in Fig. s4. The test route included the urban expressway, the main road, and the secondary main road and branch road. The driving route of the motor vehicle test was shown in Fig. s5 in the supporting information. There is no traffic light in the expressway of the third ring road, and the average speed exceeds 60 km/h. There are 10 traffic lights in the main roads, where the traffic is heavy and the traffic jams often happen. The average speed of the tested vehicle is less than  $20\\mathrm{km / h}$  when traveling on the main roads. Motor vehicles travel in different speeds under different road conditions, and present different  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rates.", "The  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rates of 6 gasoline vehicles were shown in Fig. 3a.  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rate of Vehicle  $1\\#$  increased gradually with the rising speed when the driving speed was below  $60~\\mathrm{km / h}$ . It reached a peak emission rate of  $0.0491~\\mathrm{mg / s}$  in the speed range of  $50 - 60~\\mathrm{km / h}$ , and then turned to decrease as the driving speed exceeded  $60~\\mathrm{km / h}$ .  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rates of vehicles  $2\\#$ ,  $3\\#$  and  $5\\#$  showed the similar changing trend. They increased firstly with the rising speed when the driving speed was below  $50~\\mathrm{km / h}$ . The  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rates showed a slight decrease in the speed range of  $50 - 60~\\mathrm{km / h}$ , and then continued to rise up to their maximum after the driving speed exceeded  $60~\\mathrm{km / h}$ .  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rates of vehicles  $4\\#$  and  $6\\#$  were different from those of the other 4 gasoline vehicles. No obvious changes of their  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rates were observed with the increasing driving speed. For gasoline vehicles, the average  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rate was  $0.0054~\\mathrm{mg / s}$  under low speed  $(V \\leq 20~\\mathrm{km / h})$ . It turned to  $0.0161~\\mathrm{mg / s}$  under medium speed  $(20 < V \\leq 60~\\mathrm{km / h})$ , and increased to  $0.0216~\\mathrm{mg / s}$  under high speed  $(V > 60~\\mathrm{km / h})$ . Generally, the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rate of gasoline vehicles showed a rising trend as the driving speed increased. The  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rates of 4 diesel vehicles were shown in Fig. 3b.  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rate of Vehicle  $7\\#$  increased gradually with the rising speed, and it reached peak emission rate of  $0.4814~\\mathrm{mg / s}$  in the speed range of above  $60~\\mathrm{km / h}$ .  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rates of Vehicles  $8\\#$  and  $10\\#$  showed the similar changing trend. They increased gradually with the rising speed, and reached their maximum in the speed range of above  $60~\\mathrm{km / h}$ . The  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rate of Vehicle  $9\\#$  showed a fluctuating rise with increasing speed. For diesel vehicles, the average  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rate was  $0.0879~\\mathrm{mg / s}$  under low speed  $(V \\leq 20~\\mathrm{km / h})$ . It turned to  $0.1696~\\mathrm{mg / s}$  under medium speed  $(20 < V \\leq 60~\\mathrm{km / h})$ , and increased to  $0.2084~\\mathrm{mg / s}$  under high speed  $(V > 60~\\mathrm{km / h})$ . Generally, the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rate of diesel", "The average emission rate of the tested vehicles under low speed  $(\\mathrm{V} \\leq 20 \\mathrm{~km/h})$  was  $0.0384 \\mathrm{mg/s}$ , it increased to  $0.0775 \\mathrm{mg/s}$  under medium speed  $(20 < \\mathrm{V} \\leq 60 \\mathrm{~km/h})$ , and finally reached up to  $0.0964 \\mathrm{mg/s}$  under high speed  $(\\mathrm{V} > 60 \\mathrm{~km/h})$ . Therefore, the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rate of vehicles in Wuhan was positively correlated with the driving speed of vehicles. That was, an increase in driving speed would increase the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rate of vehicles to a certain extent."], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Present Quantitative Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Line Chart"]}, "key_entities": ["PM_2.5", "gasoline vehicles", "diesel vehicles", "emission rate", "driving speed"], "concise_summary": "The figure depicts the average emission rates of PM_2.5 for gasoline and diesel vehicles across different speed segments.", "inferred_insight": "Higher driving speeds tend to increase PM_2.5 emission rates for both gasoline and diesel vehicles, indicating a positive correlation between speed and emissions.", "paper_id": 25}

metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_71", "source_md": "Does energy transition improve air quality_ Eviden.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Does energy transition improve air quality_ Eviden\\figure_4.jpg", "title": "Fig. 4. Estimated coefficients (date) prior to and following the start time of heating.", "references": ["As Fig. 4 shows, the 15 day estimator prior to and following the start of heating varied around a value of zero (0) devoid of a clear trend, and most coefficients were not statistically significant. This result indicated a similarity between pilot cities and non-pilot cities regarding AQI. Despite an abnormal increase 4-6 days following the start time of heating, such differences decreased to a negative value (as seen 2 days prior to the start time of heating), and this trend maintained for the ensuing two weeks. These results were consistent with the hypothesis that the WCHP project generated differential trends in pollutant concentrations between pilot and non-pilot cities. It is noteworthy that the period prior to the start time of heating was implemented and continued for 3-4 days in some cities prior to the formal start time of heating, which may have resulted in the early decrease. Additionally, we also estimated the Heatperiod coefficient 30 days following the start time of heating, which also indicated that effects of the WCHP project existed following the start time of heating.", "where  $Heatperiod_{i,n-1}$  is the set of dummy variables, which represents whether the  $i$ -th city is in a heating period at  $n-1$ -day.  $L_n$  represents a series of estimated coefficients 15 days prior to the start time of heating, and  $E_n$  represents a series of estimated coefficients 15 days following the start time of heating. With the omitted time category being the start time of heating,  $L_n$  captures the differences in pollutant indicators between pilot and non-pilot cities relative to differences just prior to the launch of the policy. Here we selected AQI as the air pollutant proxy. Under such specifications, if the signs of the estimated  $Heatperiod_{i,n-1}$  coefficients differ, it can be assumed that changes in pollutant concentrations are not significant.", "As a second robustness check, we constructed a counterfactual test to check whether our results could be driven by other unobserved factors. In doing so, we selected a different date during the non-heating period (May, June, July, August, September, and October) in lieu of the real start date of heating, specifying the interaction term between the treatment variable and the pseudoheating start time variable as Pseudo-HCP. The WCHP project can only take effect during the heating period, while the pseudo-HCP coefficient can reflect the impact of the WCHP project during the non-heating period. If estimated coefficients of pseudo-WCHP are significant and have the same sign as of the true WCHP shown in Table 4, we can conclude that air quality improvements in pilot cities could not be mainly attributed to the WCHP project, and the robustness of the DID model would be inadequate. In this case, improvements may be related to other pollution control policies, such as production restrictions and limits to the number of private vehicle allowed on roads.", "According to the regressions shown in Table 5, only when the start time of heating was specified in October, coefficients of pseudo-WCHP were significantly negative at a  $1\\%$  level, while other estimation results were not significant or had a positive sign. Compared to Table 4, these conflicting results failed to reject the validity of the \"WHCD\" policy. That is to say, the effect of the WCHP project was evident. In fact, we specified the heating start time on the 15th of November in consideration of the \"official\" start time of central heating while ignoring differences caused by scattered coal combustion. In October, some rural areas in the pilot and non-pilot cities began to burn scattered coal for heating, which could cause an evident increase in pollution emissions [47,48]."], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Quantitative Results", "Compare Experimental Performance"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Bar Chart"]}, "key_entities": ["AQI", "PM2.5", "SO2", "NO2", "Eastern region", "Western region", "WCHP project"], "concise_summary": "The bar chart presents the percentage distribution of AQI, PM2.5, SO2, and NO2 across all samples, and in the Eastern and Western regions.", "inferred_insight": "The figure illustrates regional differences in pollutants, supporting findings that the WCHP project impacts air quality measures across diverse geographical areas.", "paper_id": 11}

content.title: Fig. 1. Photos showing the (a) coating process and (b) sampling equipment.
content.summary: The figure shows photographs of a coating process in a workshop and the equipment used for sampling air to assess VOC emissions.
content.insight: The figure is used to illustrate the real-world context and methodology for collecting air samples to evaluate VOC exposure risks in industrial coating environments.

metadata.savepath: C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\Volatile organic compound (VOC) emissions and heal\figure_1.jpg
metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_214", "source_md": "Volatile organic compound (VOC) emissions and heal.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Volatile organic compound (VOC) emissions and heal\\figure_1.jpg", "title": "Fig. 1. Photos showing the (a) coating process and (b) sampling equipment.", "supplement": "(a) Coating process;(b) Sampling equipment", "references": ["The description of the source samples is tabulated in Table 1. Three coating sectors in China were investigated in this study, namely cargo container coating, ship coating, and furniture coating. The coating processes were divided into primary coating, secondary coating, and tertiary coating, depending on the manufacturing flows in each industrial sector. In general, there was one worker in each coating workshop under normal working conditions. The workshops were  $60 - 150\\mathrm{m}^2$  for different coating processes in these industrial sectors. The central ventilation system was installed in the workplaces and the exhaust gas was discharged from the chimney and stack. The activated carbon-based masks were used by the workers. Whole-air samples were collected in the coating workshops that were being operated under normal conditions. Fig. 1(a) shows an example of the working condition of furniture coating.", "Indoor air was collected using 3.2-L fused silica stainless steel canisters that had been precleaned with high-purity nitrogen and evacuated with an automated canister cleaner. Each sample was taken for  $30\\mathrm{min}$  using an inlet with pre-calibrated flow regulator (CS1200 sampler; Entech Instrument, Inc.) operating at  $38~\\mathrm{mL~min}^{-1}$  (Entech Instruments Inc., California, USA), as shown in Fig. 1(b). The sampling equipment was held by the sampling staff at about  $1.5\\mathrm{-m}$  high, next to the occupational painter and less than  $1\\mathrm{m}$  away from the spraying source to represent a similar distance with the painter in real situations. Three samples were collected at similar position in each workshop to ensure the representative of the field samples. Canister vacuums were checked before and after sampling. The field samples and two blank samples were sent to the Peking University (PKU) VOCs laboratory for analysis immediately after sampling within one week of shipping time. The blank", "Solvent use and paint consumption are significant source sectors of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) emissions in China. The occupational painters have high risk of health effect due to exposure to high VOCs concentration. However, the toxic components in coating environment have not been carefully identified, and the health risks of VOCs exposure have not been sufficiently assessed. This study collected air samples from nine workshops of three major coating sectors in the Yangtze River Delta of China, namely cargo container coating, ship equipment coating, and furniture coating, to evaluate the noncancer and cancer risk of toxic VOCs exposure to occupational painters under a normal working condition. The results show that the container coating had highest cancer risk  $(2.29 \\times 10^{-6} - 5.53 \\times 10^{-6})$  exceeding the safe limit of  $1.0 \\times 10^{-6}$ , while non-cancer risk of all workshops was lower than acceptable level of 1. Ethylbenzene and 1,2-dichloropropane should be targeted for priority removal during the container coating process in attempt to reduce adverse health effect on the occupational painters. This study helps better understand the health risk of VOCs exposure in coating workshops in China and provides information for policy-makers to formulate possible control of specific toxic compounds during coating process."], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Data & Experimental Setup", "sub_roles": ["Showcase Dataset Samples", "Introduce Experimental Setup"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Qualitative & Representational Imagery", "sub_types": ["Photograhp"]}, "key_entities": ["coating process", "sampling equipment", "furniture coating", "VOC emissions", "canister", "Entech Instrument"], "concise_summary": "The figure shows photographs of a coating process in a workshop and the equipment used for sampling air to assess VOC emissions.", "inferred_insight": "The figure is used to illustrate the real-world context and methodology for collecting air samples to evaluate VOC exposure risks in industrial coating environments.", "paper_id": 33}

content.title: Fig. 2. The probability bands of particle removal efficiencies of wet scrubbers for cement production (a-c) and of fabric filters for sintering (d-f), through bootstrap simulation with individual plant data.
content.summary: Figure 2 shows cumulative probability graphs of particle removal efficiencies for cement production wet scrubbers and sintering fabric filters, using bootstrap simulation data.
content.insight: The figure illustrates the efficiency variability in dust collectors, highlighting the application of simulation to estimate PM removal rates across different particle sizes for industrial processes.

metadata.keywords: ["particle removal efficiencies", "wet scrubbers", "cement production", "fabric filters", "sintering", "bootstrap simulation", "PM2.5", "PM2.5-10", "TSP"]
metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_263", "source_md": "Quantifying the uncertainties of China's emission.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Quantifying the uncertainties of China's emission\\figure_2.jpg", "title": "Fig. 2. The probability bands of particle removal efficiencies of wet scrubbers for cement production (a-c) and of fabric filters for sintering (d-f), through bootstrap simulation with individual plant data.", "supplement": "(a)  PM_{2.5}  (WET for CEM);(b)  PM_{2.5 - 10}  (WET for CEM);(c)  PM_{>10}  (WET for CEM);(d)  PM_{2.5}  (FF for sintering);(e)  PM_{2.5 - 10}  (FF for sintering);(f)  PM_{>10}  (FF for sintering)", "references": ["The removal efficiencies of dust collector by particle size are crucial for estimating the size-fractioned PM emissions of single plant. Nevertheless, there was little information on removal rates of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  or  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  but TSP for individual plants in current databases, and limited field measurements across the country had to be relied on (Zhao et al., 2010). In this study, the investigated removal rates for TSP by plant and the PM size fractions by dust collector type from previous work were incorporated to calculate the removal rates for size-fractioned PM by plant. For data samples that passed K-S test, bootstrap simulations were applied to quantify the mean values and  $95\\%$  CIs of the removal rates by dust collector type and particle size. Fig. 2 provides bootstrap simulation examples for particle removal efficiencies of wet scrubber for cement production and those of fabric filter for sintering. For those that failed to pass K-S test, normal and uniform distributions were subjectively applied for the parameters with sample size  $\\geq 10$  and  $< 10$ , respectively. The  $95\\%$  CIs were calculated based on the relative ranges provided in our previous work by dust collector type (Zhao et al., 2010). Therefore, the PDFs of PM removal rates were obtained based on the data of individual plants but were applied by dust collector type at sector level, as summarized in Table S3 in the supplement."], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Quantitative Results", "Showcase Prediction Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Line Chart"]}, "key_entities": ["particle removal efficiencies", "wet scrubbers", "cement production", "fabric filters", "sintering", "bootstrap simulation", "PM2.5", "PM2.5-10", "TSP"], "concise_summary": "Figure 2 shows cumulative probability graphs of particle removal efficiencies for cement production wet scrubbers and sintering fabric filters, using bootstrap simulation data.", "inferred_insight": "The figure illustrates the efficiency variability in dust collectors, highlighting the application of simulation to estimate PM removal rates across different particle sizes for industrial processes.", "paper_id": 28}

content.title: Fig. 3. reduction effects of the WCHP project on major pollutants in eastern and western region.
content.summary: The figure illustrates the reduction percentages of major pollutants due to the WCHP project, depicted through changes in central heating areas and coal consumption from 1997 to 2017.
content.insight: The figure demonstrates the effectiveness of the WCHP project in reducing air pollutants, highlighting the reduction in coal dependence due to increased central heating coverage, primarily benefiting air quality in both eastern and western regions.

{"id": "img_70", "source_md": "Does energy transition improve air quality_ Eviden.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\Does energy transition improve air quality_ Eviden\figure_3.jpg", "title": "Fig. 3. reduction effects of the WCHP project on major pollutants in eastern and western region.", "references": ["In order to intuitively illustrate the effects of the WCHP project on air pollutants, we separately calculated percentage reduction effects of this policy in the eastern and western regions (Fig. 3). In the eastern region, decreasing percentages of AQI, $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , and $\mathrm{NO}_2$ were $15.51\%$ , $14.72\%$ , $71.08\%$ , and $31.22\%$ , respectively. In the western region, corresponding decreasing percentages were $25.02\%$ , $22.25\%$ , $65.43\%$ , and $36.49\%$ , respectively. Decreases of most", "Coal used for heating rural households poses a severe threat to ambient air pollution and human health. In 2017, China implemented the Winter Clean Heating Pilot (WCHP) project, which was designed to convert energy from "coal-to-gas/electricity". However, the impact of the WCHP project and its associated dynamic changes have not yet been well quantified. Based on daily monitored panel data covering 35 pilot cities, we conducted a difference-in-differences (DID) model to identify whether or not the WCHP project is effective in maintaining air quality. Results showed that AQI, $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ , and $\mathrm{SO}_2$ emissions decreased by $20.4\%$ , $18.59\%$ , $34.1\%$ , and $68.4\%$ , respectively, and, except for $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , these pollutant reductions were greater in Western China than in Eastern China. Furthermore, when certain variables were controlled, such as weather, holidays, GDP, the ratio between secondary industries, income levels, and environmental expenditures, these results were also robust. Parallel trends and placebo tests were additionally used to confirm the reliability of the DID model. The implementation of energy transition could gain the synergy between emission reduction and air quality improvement. Policy recommendations were finally made on rural-scale energy transition, including reforming the cost of electricity/gas through advanced technology and subsidies to account for regional inequality.", "Table 4 provides the pollutant reduction effects under the WCHP project using the DID model. As a weighted-average indicator of primary air pollutants, AQI can objectively represent the degree of air pollution [40]. $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ had the highest percentage increment derived from scattered coal combustion during winter heating periods. $\mathrm{SO}_2$ and $\mathrm{NO_x}$ were the main components of acid rain. Hence, we selected AQI, $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , and $\mathrm{NO_x}$ as explanatory variables. Results showed that AQI significantly increased (42.79) during periods when heating was employed, while the WCHP project decreased AQI by 8.72, offsetting approximately $20.38\%$ of the negative impacts caused by winter heating. Likewise, $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ concentrations decreased by $18.59\%$ due to the WCHP project. This finding indicated that the WCHP project has had a significant reduction effect on air pollution during winter heating periods, particularly for $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , since $\mathrm{SO}_2$ emissions decreased significantly by $68.36\%$ . However, the reduction effect on $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ remained far from the percentage released by the official feedback report (i.e., $33.7\%$ ). These results indicated that the net effect was smaller than the", "

content.title: Fig. 8. Correlation coefficients between air quality change and socioeconomic factors.
content.summary: Fig. 8 shows correlation coefficients between air quality change and socioeconomic factors, indicating low multicollinearity and relationships among the variables.
content.insight: The figure highlights that socioeconomic factors, such as environmental governance, play a key role in air quality improvement, with no high correlations reducing reliability concerns of the analysis.

metadata.full_json:
{"id": "img_29", "source_md": "Air quality changes in China 2013-2020_ Effectiven.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\Air quality changes in China 2013-2020_ Effectiven\figure_8.jpg", "title": "Fig. 8. Correlation coefficients between air quality change and socioeconomic factors.", "references": ["The Kendall correlation coefficients depicted the association between air quality change and socioeconomic factors, as shown in Fig. 8. Ordinary least squares analysis was also done to assess the variance inflation factors, which ranged from 1.007 to 2.297 and were all less than the threshold of 10, indicating that there was no multiple collinearity issue and no high correlation among socioeconomic variables, which made the subsequent correlation analysis more reliable.", "As depicted in Fig. 8, the socioeconomic factors were seen not to be highly associated with the change rate of AQI. Amongst, an obvious observation was that the increase in environmental governance and a decrease in industrialization were positively related to air quality improvement. Furthermore, the increase in economic development,", "Clean and efficient use of coal in China entered the implementation stage in 2015, and a series of policies were established to promote the sustainable development of clean coal technologies (CCTs). However, the long-term effects of the clean coal technology policies (CCTPs) have not been evaluated, and the outbreak of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) would bring great uncertainty regarding the effectiveness evaluation. Employing deep learning approaches and spatial autocorrelation analysis, the present work intended to explore the air quality variation before and after the implementation of CCTPs, and investigate the association between air quality and socioeconomic factors to explore the internal mechanism of air quality improvement. Results showed that after implementing CCTPs, the air quality index (AQI) had an average reduction of  $18.82\%$  and the identical drop in air pollution in 2018 implied a 2-year time lag of CCTPs. Additionally, emission reduction and deindustrialization were explored as two promising ways to improve air quality while promoting energy transformation. The findings indicated that the Chinese government ought to pay more attention to long-term plans; industrial restructuring and environmental protection should be insisted upon to realize sustainable energy development. By providing a perspective on air quality improvement after the policies are implemented, this study can lead practitioners and academia to a comprehensive and objective view of clean coal policies.", "Different from the previous policy effectiveness studies, which focused on a specific region or policy with little to national and industrial overview, the main goal of this study is to systematically and comprehensively measure the air quality variation after the implementation of CCTPs at the national level. Based on China's air quality and meteorological data from 2013 to 2019, deep learning approaches were applied to forecast the air quality in 2020, eliminating the uncertainties that COVID-19 brought about. Then, the spatial autocorrelation analysis was utilized to investigate the temporal and spatial distribution characteristics of air pollution. And applying socioeconomic data, the association between AQI change and socioeconomic factors was calculated to explore the most likely mechanism of CCTPs. Our findings showed that there was a significant air quality improvement after the implementation of CCTPs, indicating that clean coal transformation adapts to the requirements of sustainable development in China. And two main channels to reduce air pollution were excavated, namely industrialization and environmental governance. This is essential to reaching an agreement on clean coal utilization, supporting the formulation of energy policies, and combating climate change.", "related policies have reached a consensus. Nevertheless, the contributions mainly focus on regions with high-polluting and energy-intensive industries or target a single kind of clean coal policy. Energy transition is a systematic initiative that requires a long-term and holistic vision to guide nations, industries, companies, and individuals to collaborate from all aspects (Wei et al., 2022). The efficient and effective implementation of CCTPs also emphasizes the interrelatedness of social, technical, institutional and political changes (Markard, 2018), and sets the tone for atmospheric administration in a certain period. Therefore, this paper investigates the effectiveness of CCTPs at the national level with the industrial background of coal-based clean energy, and pays attention to how air quality varied after the implementation of CCTPs. Second, in the existing studies, the mechanism of energy transition policies on environmental pollution control is rarely discussed. By clarifying the association between the AQI and socioeconomic factors, this study tries to provide quantitative results for the internal mechanism of air quality improvement.", "where  $C_{AQI}$  is the change rate of the AQI for each studied city,  $pr_{AQI}$  is the average AQI of each month for the studied cities from 2013 to 2015, and  $po_{AQI}$  is the average AQI of each month from 2016 to 2020. The calculated results vary between  $-100\%$  and  $+100\%$ , where negative and positive values indicate improvement or deterioration of air quality, and 0 represents no change.", "To comprehensively explore the association between air quality change and socioeconomic factors, and reflect the internal mechanism of CCTPs working on air quality improvement, the correlation coefficients were calculated with all socioeconomic factors separately using Equation (13). Ordinary least squares regression was also carried out, and the variance inflation factor of each parameter was computed to verify the existence of multicollinearity.", "To better understand the policy effect on air quality improvement, the change rate of AQI was computed between the pre- and post-policy nodes, as shown in Table 4. All the areas experienced improved air quality, with the change rate of AQI ranging from  $4.28\%$  to  $29.54\%$ . Regionally, the degree of improvement was maximum in central China  $(25.61\%)$ , followed by northeast  $(25.47\%)$ , eastern  $(20.88\%)$ , and northern China  $(18.30\%)$ . The three districts with the least improvement were northwest, southern, and southwest China, with change rates of  $15.27\%$ $14.63\%$  and  $14.44\%$  respectively. On average, the AQI value was reduced by  $18.82\%$  from 2013 to 2020, indicating that China's air quality was greatly improved after the implementation of CCTPs.", "During the period of CCTPs implementation, an obvious air quality improvement was observed in the Chinese mainland, manifested as a decrease of AQI value by  $18.82\%$  from 2013 to 2020. It is concluded that the clean and efficient transformation of coal resources conforms to China's national conditions, and could indeed improve the atmospheric environment, which has been demonstrated in the existing research as well (Li and Patino-Echeverri, 2017; Liu et al., 2021). Specifically, correlation coefficients showed that the promotion of emission reduction and deindustrialization could fundamentally help to reduce airborne contamination, although their persistence relies on continued efforts and is influenced by various factors (Yang and Teng, 2018). Another important observation was a 2-year time lag before the stringent CCTPs starting from 2015 takes any strong positive effects, shown as a dramatical reduction of AQI in 2018. It implies that the influence of time lag should be considered in the decision-making process, as a time lag commonly appears in air pollution regulations and plans (Van der et al., 2017).", "Limitations in both data acquisition and method selection should be acknowledged. First, the air quality variation before and after the implementation of CCTPs was investigated over a time span of 7 years, owing to the AQI data is available since 2013. The future study would be enriched with broader periods to observe a long-term performance. For method selection, the first limitation is that only CNN and LSTM were applied, ensemble-based approaches would be explored and incorporated along with the proposed model for further improvement in the prediction performance of the proposed framework. Further, the five socioeconomic factors were extracted and analyzed based on existing research and empirical knowledge. Future studies should continue to examine the deeper mechanisms of CCTPs using more quantitative and modelling methods.", "Renew. Sustain.
